Abstract: Subtle visual anomalies such as hairline cracks, sub-millimeter voids, and low-contrast inclusions are structurally atypical yet visually ambiguous, making them both difficult to annotate and easy to overlook during active learning. Standard acquisition heuristics based on discriminative uncertainty or feature diversity often overselect dominant patterns while underexploring sparse yet important regions of the data space. This failure mode is especially severe in industrial defect inspection, where anomalies may be both low-prevalence and difficult to distinguish from surrounding structure. To resolve this, we propose GSAL, an active learning framework for object detection that combines a diffusion-based difficulty signal with a hierarchical semantic coverage prior. The diffusion component scores images and proposals using reconstruction discrepancy and denoising variability, prioritizing visually atypical or ambiguous examples. However, diffusion alone does not prevent acquisition from repeatedly favoring hard samples within dominant semantic modes. The semantic component therefore organizes candidate samples in a three-level concept graph and promotes coverage of underrepresented semantic regions while providing interpretable acquisition rationales. By balancing visual difficulty with semantic coverage, GSAL improves retrieval of subtle and rare targets that are often missed by uncertainty-only selection. Experiments on a proprietary thin-film defect, Pascal VOC and MS COCO dataset show consistent gains in label efficiency and rare-class retrieval over uncertainty-, diversity-, and hybrid-based baselines

</details>


### [263] [Efficient Image Annotation via Semi-Supervised Object Segmentation with Label Propagation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.22992)
*Vitalii Tutevych,Raphael Memmesheimer,Luca Eichler,Dmytro Pavlichenko,Fynn Schilke,Rodja Krudewig,Sven Behnke*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper introduces a semi-supervised label propagation method for segmenting household objects using a combination of mask proposals and Hopfield networks within foundation model embedding spaces.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the challenges of poor generalization in open-vocabulary detectors and time-intensive annotations in fully supervised training of object detectors, especially for service robots in constrained settings.

Method: The method uses a segment proposer for class-agnostic masks and combines foundation model embedding spaces (CLIP, ViT, Theia) with Hopfield networks for semi-supervised label propagation.

Result: Scaling to 50 object classes is achieved with limited annotations, and 60% of data can be automatically labeled in settings with preparation constraints, like RoboCup@Home.

Conclusion: The proposed method reduces annotation overhead, improves scalability for household object segmentation, and is made accessible with dataset and code shared publicly.

Abstract: Reliable object perception is necessary for general-purpose service robots. Open-vocabulary detectors struggle to generalize beyond a few classes and fully supervised training of object detectors requires time-intensive annotations. We present a semi-supervised label propagation approach for household object segmentation. A segment proposer generates class-agnostic masks, and an ensemble of Hopfield networks assigns labels by learning representative embeddings in complementary foundation model embedding spaces (CLIP, ViT, Theia). Our approach scales to 50 object classes with limited annotation overhead and can automatically label 60% of the data in a RoboCup@Home setting, where preparation time is severely constrained. Dataset and code are publicly available at https://github.com/ais-bonn/label_propagation.

</details>


### [264] [GenAssets: Generating in-the-wild 3D Assets in Latent Space](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.23010)
*Ze Yang,Jingkang Wang,Haowei Zhang,Sivabalan Manivasagam,Yun Chen,Raquel Urtasun*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper proposes a 3D latent diffusion model to generate complete and high-quality traffic participant assets from in-the-wild LiDAR and camera data, outperforming existing methods in simulation asset creation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: High-quality, diverse, and realistic 3D assets are vital for multi-sensor simulations in autonomy development. However, existing methods are limited in speed, viewpoint diversity, and performance on in-the-wild driving scenes with occluded and sparse data.

Method: The approach uses a "reconstruct-then-generate" pipeline: firstly, occlusion-aware neural rendering is employed to create a high-quality latent space for objects, followed by training a diffusion model that operates in this latent space.

Result: The method produces high-quality 3D assets with complete geometry and appearance from challenging in-the-wild data, surpassing the performance of existing reconstruction and generation techniques.

Conclusion: Combining occlusion-aware neural rendering with latent diffusion modeling provides a scalable and effective solution for generating realistic 3D assets for autonomous driving simulation.

Abstract: High-quality 3D assets for traffic participants are critical for multi-sensor simulation, which is essential for the safe end-to-end development of autonomy. Building assets from in-the-wild data is key for diversity and realism, but existing neural-rendering based reconstruction methods are slow and generate assets that render well only from viewpoints close to the original observations, limiting their usefulness in simulation. Recent diffusion-based generative models build complete and diverse assets, but perform poorly on in-the-wild driving scenes, where observed actors are captured under sparse and limited fields of view, and are partially occluded. In this work, we propose a 3D latent diffusion model that learns on in-the-wild LiDAR and camera data captured by a sensor platform and generates high-quality 3D assets with complete geometry and appearance. Key to our method is a "reconstruct-then-generate" approach that first leverages occlusion-aware neural rendering trained over multiple scenes to build a high-quality latent space for objects, and then trains a diffusion model that operates on the latent space. We show our method outperforms existing reconstruction and generation based methods, unlocking diverse and scalable content creation for simulation.

</details>


### [265] [AmaraSpatial-10K: A Spatially and Semantically Aligned 3D Dataset for Spatial Computing and Embodied AI](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.23018)
*Mohammad Sadegh Salehi,Alex Perkins,Igor Maurell,Ashkan Dabbagh,Raymond Wong*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper presents AmaraSpatial-10K, a dataset of 10,000 high-quality 3D assets crafted for practical downstream applications such as AI, VR, and robotics. The dataset includes well-formatted 3D models with strong evaluation metrics that outperform existing resources.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation behind this paper is to address the lack of deployment-ready 3D assets within web-scale collections, which tend to have issues such as wrong scaling, poor geometry, and limited texturing. These issues hinder their usability in practical, high-stakes applications like robotics, simulations, and AR/VR.

Method: The authors built AmaraSpatial-10K, a dataset with metric-scaled 3D models featuring PBR material maps, collision hulls, metadata, and reference images. They also introduced a robust evaluation suite (SPS, anchor-error metric, etc.) to test and verify the dataset's readiness compared to existing sources.

Result: AmaraSpatial-10K demonstrated superior performance in text-based retrieval and various quality evaluations. For example, the dataset achieved a CLIP Recall@5 score of 0.612, a significant improvement over Objaverse assets, and showed its capability for physics-aware and contextually meaningful applications.

Conclusion: The paper concludes that AmaraSpatial-10K substantially addresses common limitations in existing 3D asset datasets, setting a new benchmark for practical use in embodied AI and AR/VR contexts. The open-source availability further ensures its impact and accessibility for the community.

Abstract: Web-scale 3D asset collections are abundant, but rarely deployment-ready. Assets ship with arbitrary metric scale, incorrect pivots and forward axes, brittle geometry, and textures that do not support relighting, which limits their utility for embodied AI, robotics simulation, game development, and AR/VR. We present AmaraSpatial-10K, a dataset of over 10,000 synthetic 3D assets designed for downstream use rather than volume alone. Each asset is released as a metric-scaled, semantically anchored .glb with separated PBR material maps, a convex collision hull, a paired reference image, and rich multi-sentence text metadata. The dataset spans indoor objects, vehicles, architecture, creatures, and props under a unified spatial convention. Alongside the dataset, we introduce an evaluation suite for 3D asset banks. The suite comprises a continuous Scale Plausibility Score (SPS) with an LLM-as-Judge interval protocol, an LLM Concept Density score for metadata, an anchor-error metric, and a cross-modal CLIP coherence protocol, and we use it to audit AmaraSpatial-10K alongside matched subsets from Objaverse, HSSD, ABO, and GSO. Compared with Objaverse-sourced assets, we demonstrate that AmaraSpatial-10K substantially improves text-based retrieval precision (CLIP Recall@5 of 0.612 vs 0.181, a 3.4x improvement with median rank falling from 267 to 3), and we establish that it satisfies the spatial and semantic prerequisites for physics-aware scene composition and embodied-AI asset banks, leaving those downstream evaluations to future work. AmaraSpatial-10K is publicly available on Hugging Face.

</details>


### [266] [Understanding Representation Gaps Across Scales in Tropical Tree Species Classification from Drone Imagery](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.23019)
*Sulagna Saha,Arthur Ouaknine,Etienne Laliberté,Carol Altimas,Evan M. Gora,Adriane Esquivel Muelbert,Ian R. McGregor,Cesar Gutierrez,Vanessa E. Rubio,David Rolnick*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper evaluates tropical tree species classification using UAV imagery, comparing high-res close-up images with coarser top-view images, and suggests aligning visual representations to enhance performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The study aims to address the challenge of accurately classifying tropical tree species due to high biodiversity and visual similarity, leveraging recent improvements in UAV data acquisition.

Method: The researchers conducted fine-tuning experiments using paired top-view and close-up UAV imagery from tropical forests, comparing vision foundation models and domain-specific plant recognition tools.

Result: Close-up images consistently outperformed top-view aerial imagery in classification, especially for rare species. Self-supervised alignment across scales shows promise for better integration.

Conclusion: Incorporating high-resolution close-up UAV imagery could significantly enhance biodiversity monitoring in tropical forests, especially if multi-scale visual data is effectively aligned.

Abstract: Accurate classification of tropical tree species from unoccupied aerial vehicle (UAV) imagery remains challenging due to high species diversity and strong visual similarity among species at typical image resolutions (centimeters per pixel). In contrast, models trained on close-up citizen science photographs captured with smartphones achieve strong plant species classification performance. Recent advances in UAV data acquisition now enable the collection of close-up images that are spatially registered with top-view aerial imagery and approach the level of visual detail found in smartphone photographs, with the trade-off that such high-resolution photos cannot be acquired for many trees. In this work, we evaluate the performance of existing methods using paired top-view and close-up UAV imagery collected in a species-rich tropical forest. Through fine-tuning experiments, we quantify the performance gap between vision foundation models and in-domain generalist plant recognition models across both image types (high-resolution close-up versus coarser-resolution top-view imagery). We show that classification performance is consistently higher on close-up images than on top-view aerial imagery, and that this performance gap widens for rare species. Finally, we propose that self-supervised representation alignment across these two spatial scales offers a promising approach for integrating fine-grained visual information into canopy-level species classification models based on top-view UAV imagery. Leveraging high-resolution close-up UAV imagery to enhance canopy-level species classification could substantially improve large-scale monitoring of tropical forest biodiversity.

</details>


### [267] [Urban Flood Observations (UFO): A hand-labeled training and validation dataset of post-flood inundation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.23066)
*Rohit Mukherjee,Hannah K. Friedrich,Beth Tellman,Ariful Islam,Zhijie Zhang,Jonathan Giezendanner,Upmanu Lall,Venkataraman Lakshmi*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This study introduces "Urban Flood Observations" (UFO), a global dataset designed for mapping urban flooding using satellite imagery. It outperforms standard methods in segmentation accuracy and is publicly available for further research.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Urban flooding is a global challenge, and existing satellite methods struggle with low resolution, infrequent imaging, and cloud disruptions, creating a need for improved inundation mapping.

Method: The researchers compiled and hand-labeled a dataset called UFO with 215 high-resolution image chips from 14 global flood events. They annotated images into 'inundated' and 'non-inundated' classes and trained a segmentation model using leave-one-event-out cross-validation.

Result: The segmentation model achieved a mean IoU of 77.3%. By contrast, existing methods like NASA IMPACT and Google Dynamic World returned IoUs of 44.1% and 48.1%, respectively, illustrating the dataset's superior performance.

Conclusion: The UFO dataset is a valuable, publicly available resource for advancing urban flood detection from satellite imagery, addressing the limitations of earlier methods and enabling more reliable inundation mapping.

Abstract: Urban flooding affects lives and infrastructure worldwide. Mapping inundation in complex urban environments from satellite imagery remains challenging due to limited spatial resolution, infrequent acquisitions, and cloud cover. We present Urban Flood Observations (UFO), a global, hand-labeled dataset of post-flood inundation in diverse urban settings. UFO comprises 215 image chips (1024 by 1024 pixels) from 14 flood events between 2017 and 2021, derived from 3 m PlanetScope imagery. Each chip is annotated with two classes: 'inundated' (all visible surface water, including floodwater and pre-existing water bodies (permanent or seasonal)) and 'non-inundated'. To demonstrate the dataset's utility, we trained a segmentation model using leave-one-event-out cross-validation, achieving a mean Intersection over Union (IoU) of 77.3. We also used UFO to evaluate two widely used surface water products, the Sentinel-1-based NASA IMPACT model and Google's 10 m Dynamic World water class, which yielded IoUs of 44.1 and 48.1, respectively. UFO is publicly available to support the development and validation of urban inundation mapping methods.

</details>


### [268] [From Pixels to Explanations: Interpretable Diabetic Retinopathy Grading with CNN-Transformer Ensembles, Visual Explainability and Vision-Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.23079)
*Pir Bakhsh Khokhar,Carmine Gravino,Fabio Palomba,Sule Yildirim Yayilgan,Sarang Shaikh*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The study combines deep learning models with multimodal explanations for DR severity screening, achieving high accuracy and clinical-grade interpretability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To improve both grading accuracy and interpretability of diabetic retinopathy screening in clinical settings.

Method: Six CNN and transformer-based classifiers were tested with ensembling strategies and hybrid fusion; interpretability was assessed via Grad-CAM++ maps and vision-language model rationales.

Result: Modern CNNs performed best with QWK up to 0.919; ensembling boosted ordinal agreement, especially weighted soft voting (QWK 0.934).

Conclusion: The methodology enhances screening quality with accurate grading and interpretable multimodal outputs, but hybrid fusion showed no significant statistical improvement.

Abstract: The quality of diabetic retinopathy (DR) screening relies on the ability to correctly grade severity; however, many deep-learning (DL) classifiers cannot be easily interpreted in the clinical context. This study presents a methodology that combines strong discriminative models with multimodal explanations, converting retinal pixels into clinically interpretable outputs. Using the APTOS 2019 benchmark, we evaluated six representative CNN- and transformer-based backbones under a controlled protocol with stratified five-fold cross-validation. We then compared ensembling strategies (hard voting, weighted soft voting, stacking) and investigated a hybrid class-level fusion variant to exploit grade-specific advantages. For interpretability, we produced Grad-CAM++ visual attribution maps and short textual rationales using vision-language models (VLMs) conditioned on the fundus image and classifier outputs under conservative prompting constraints. Modern CNN backbones (ResNet-50 and ConvNeXt-Tiny) provided the strongest single-model baselines, with cross-validated QWK up to 0.919 and 0.914, respectively. Ensembling improved ordinal agreement, and weighted soft voting was the most consistent across folds (QWK 0.934 +/- 0.017). Hybrid class-level fusion was competitive but did not yield a statistically reliable improvement over standard fusion in paired fold comparisons (Holm-adjusted p >= 1.000). For explanation quality, Grad-CAM++ offered plausible but coarse localization, and VLM rationales were generally grade-consistent. Quantitatively, VLM variants showed a trade-off between clinical completeness and template-level semantic similarity (coverage 0.700 vs. BERTScore 0.072), while image-text alignment was comparable (CLIPScore approximately 0.34).

</details>


### [269] [Toward Real-World Adoption of Portrait Relighting via Hybrid Domain Knowledge Fusion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.23094)
*Qian Huang,Mayoore Selvarasa Jaiswal,Zhen Zhong,Rochelle Pereira,Jianyuan Min*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper introduces a method to enhance portrait relighting by combining synthetic, OLAT, and real-world datasets into a single efficient model, achieving faster inference and high-quality results.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation arises from the need to overcome dataset domain gaps, camera variability, and computational inefficiencies that hinder practical portrait relighting adoption.

Method: The method involves Hybrid Domain Knowledge Fusion, utilizing specialized prior models adapted to specific domains and knowledge distillation into a lightweight student model for multi-domain expertise. A large synthetic dataset supports the training pipeline.

Result: The approach achieves a significant inference speedup (6x to 240x) with state-of-the-art visual quality.

Conclusion: This hybrid model successfully reduces computational costs while maintaining high visual quality, promoting real-world adoption of portrait relighting.

Abstract: The real-world adoption of portrait relighting is hindered by dataset domain gaps, camera sensitivity, and computational costs. We address these challenges with Hybrid Domain Knowledge Fusion, a paradigm that fuses the specialized strengths of synthetic, One-Light-at-A-Time (OLAT), and real-world datasets into a compact model. Our approach features specialized prior models hardened by domain-aware adaptation, followed by augmented knowledge distillation into a lightweight student model with multi-domain expertise. Our method demonstrates a 6x to 240x inference speedup while maintaining state-of-the-art (SOTA) visual quality in the experiments. Additionally, we construct a massive, high-fidelity synthetic dataset with diverse ground-truth intrinsics to support our training pipeline.

</details>


### [270] [INSIGHT: Indoor Scene Intelligence from Geometric-Semantic Hierarchy Transfer for Public~Safety](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.23095)
*Alexander Nikitas Dimopoulos,Joseph Grasso,John Beltz*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper introduces INSIGHT, a system for mapping 2D image intelligence into 3D space to overcome labeled indoor data scarcity and improve recognition of safety-critical features.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Indoor spaces lack machine-readable maps of safety equipment, posing challenges for first responders and public safety. There is also a scarcity of labeled indoor training data and poor detection of small safety-critical features in existing methods.

Method: INSIGHT uses a zero-target-domain-annotation pipeline that employs registered RGB-D data to project 2D image understanding into 3D space. It includes two vision stacks: SAM3 for text-prompted segmentation and a traditional computer vision stack for detection tasks. Outputs are compressed for fast data transmission.

Result: INSIGHT generates labeled point clouds and compact ISO-compliant scene graphs, achieving high per-point labeling accuracy and effective detection of safety-critical classes. Payloads transmit quickly, addressing the bottleneck of data labeling.

Conclusion: The pipeline successfully transfers 2D semantic understanding into 3D space, alleviates data annotation challenges, and provides compact building intelligence solutions suitable for field deployment.

Abstract: Indoor environments lack the spatial intelligence infrastructure that GPS provides outdoors; first responders arriving at unfamiliar buildings typically have no machine-readable map of safety equipment. Prior work on 3D semantic segmentation for public safety identified two barriers: scarcity of labeled indoor training data and poor recognition of small safety-critical features by native point-cloud methods. This paper presents INSIGHT, a zero-target-domain-annotation pipeline that projects 2D image understanding into 3D metric space via registered RGB-D data. Two interchangeable vision stacks share a common 3D back end: a SAM3 foundation-model stack for text-prompted segmentation, and a traditional CV stack (open-set detection, VQA, OCR) whose intermediate outputs are independently inspectable. Evaluated on all seven subareas of Stanford 2D-3D-S (70{,}496 images), the pipeline produces Pointcept-schema-compatible labeled point clouds and ISO~19164-compliant scene graphs with ${\sim}10^{4}{\times}$ compression; role-filtered payloads transmit in ${<}15$\,s at 1\,Mbps over FirstNet Band~14. We report per-point labeling accuracy on 7 shared classes, detection sensitivity for 15 safety-critical classes absent from public 3D benchmarks alongside code-capped deployable estimates, and inter-pipeline complementarity, demonstrating that 2D-to-3D semantic transfer addresses the labeled-data bottleneck while scene graphs provide building intelligence compact enough for field deployment.

</details>


### [271] [Keypoint-based Dynamic Object 6-DoF Pose Tracking via Event Camera](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.23387)
*Zhe Wang,Qijin Song,Zihao Li,Jingyu Xiao,Weibang Bai*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper introduces a novel event camera-based method for 6-DoF dynamic object pose estimation, using keypoint detection, continuous tracking, and the EPnP algorithm.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Challenges like motion blur, sensor noise, and low-light conditions in conventional camera-based dynamic object pose estimation hinder their effectiveness. Event cameras present an alternative to overcome these limitations.

Method: A keypoint detection network identifies keypoints from event streams. Continuous tracking uses event polarity, spatial coordinates, and event density. Hash mapping and EPnP algorithm enable the final 6-DoF pose estimation.

Result: Experimental results show superior accuracy and robustness of this method over state-of-the-art event camera techniques in both simulated and real environments.

Conclusion: The proposed event camera-based approach provides an accurate and robust solution for dynamic object pose estimation, advancing robot manipulation tasks.

Abstract: Accurate 6-DoF pose estimation of objects is critical for robots to perform precise manipulation tasks. However, for dynamic object pose estimation, conventional camera-based approaches face several major challenges, such as motion blur, sensor noise, and low-light limitation. To address these issues, we employ event cameras, whose high dynamic range and low latency offer a promising solution. Furthermore, we propose a keypoint-based detection and tracking approach for dynamic object pose estimation. Firstly, a keypoint detection network is constructed to extract keypoints from the time surface generated by the event stream. Subsequently, the polarity and spatial coordinates of the events are leveraged, and the event density in the vicinity of each keypoint is utilized to achieve continuous keypoint tracking. Finally, a hash mapping is established between the 2D keypoints and the 3D model keypoints, and the EPnP algorithm is employed to estimate the 6-DoF pose. Experimental results demonstrate that, whether in simulated or real event environments, the proposed method outperforms the event-based state-of-the-art methods in terms of both accuracy and robustness.

</details>


### [272] [Transferable Physical-World Adversarial Patches Against Object Detection in Autonomous Driving](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.23105)
*Zihui Zhu,Ziqi Zhou,Yichen Wang,Lulu Xue,Minghui Li,Shengshan Hu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: AdvAD is a new transfer-based attack framework that generates physical adversarial patches to successfully and robustly fool object detectors in autonomous driving, surpassing current methods in effectiveness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the poor transferability of physical adversarial patch attacks against multiple object detectors, which is a critical concern in improving the safety of autonomous driving systems.

Method: AdvAD develops a unified optimization framework that generates adversarial patches using multiple detection models. It incorporates adaptive balancing of model contributions, robustness to physical variations through augmentations, and geometric transformations to ensure consistent attack success.

Result: AdvAD exhibits superior transferability and performance in fooling both digital and real-world object detectors, outperforming state-of-the-art adversarial attack techniques.

Conclusion: The work highlights AdvAD's capability as a powerful and effective transfer-based adversarial attack against object detection, revealing shared vulnerabilities across detectors and emphasizing the need for defense mechanisms in autonomous driving systems.

Abstract: Deep learning drives major advances in autonomous driving (AD), where object detectors are central to perception. However, adversarial attacks pose significant threats to the reliability and safety of these systems, with physical adversarial patches representing a particularly potent form of attack. Physical adversarial patch attacks pose severe risks but are usually crafted for a single model, yielding poor transferability to unseen detectors. We propose AdvAD, a transfer-based physical attack against object detection in autonomous driving. Instead of targeting a specific detector, AdvAD optimizes adversarial patches over multiple detection models in a unified framework, encouraging the learned perturbations to capture shared vulnerabilities across architectures. The optimization process adaptively balances model contributions and enforces robustness to physical variations. It further employs data augmentation and geometric transformations to maintain patch effectiveness under diverse physical conditions. Experiments in both digital and real-world settings show that AdvAD consistently outperforms state-of-the-art (SOTA) attacks in performance and transferability.

</details>


### [273] [Learning from Imperfect Text Guidance: Robust Long-Tail Visual Recognition with High-Noise Label](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.23125)
*Mengke Li,Haiquan Ling,Yiqun Zhang,Yang Lu,Hui Huang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper proposes Weak Teacher Supervision (WTS) utilizing pre-trained visual-language models to address label-image mismatches in noisy and long-tailed datasets, achieving superior performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to solve the issue of label-image mismatch in real-world datasets, where traditional methods struggle, especially under high label noise and long-tail data distributions.

Method: Using auxiliary text information from labels via pre-trained visual-language models to correct label-image mismatches. The technique, Weak Teacher Supervision (WTS), works by comparing text-predicted labels with observed labels.

Result: WTS outperforms traditional methods in synthetic and real-world datasets, enhancing accuracy under high-noise conditions. The methodology proves robust against label noise and distribution bias.

Conclusion: The study establishes WTS as an effective approach for addressing label-image inconsistencies and improving the robustness of deep learning models in complex datasets.

Abstract: Real-world data often exhibit long-tailed distributions with numerous noisy labels, substantially degrading the performance of deep models. While prior research has made progress in addressing this combined challenge, it overlooks the severe label-image mismatch inherent to high-noise settings, thereby limiting their effectiveness. Given that observed labels, though mismatched with images, still retain category information, we propose employing auxiliary text information from labels to address label-image inconsistencies in long-tailed noisy data. Specifically, we leverage the intrinsic cross-modal alignment in pre-trained visual-language models to correct the label-image inconsistencies. This supervisory signal, referred to as Weak Teacher Supervision (WTS), is unaffected by label noise and data distribution biases, albeit exhibits limited accuracy. Therefore, the activation of WTS is determined by evaluating the discrepancy between text-predicted labels and observed labels. Extensive experiments demonstrate the superior performance of WTS across synthetic and real-world datasets, particularly under high-noise conditions. The source code is available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/WTS-0F3C.

</details>


### [274] [Learning to Identify Out-of-Distribution Objects for 3D LiDAR Anomaly Segmentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.23604)
*Simone Mosco,Daniel Fusaro,Alberto Pretto*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A new approach for 3D LiDAR anomaly segmentation in autonomous driving and robotics is proposed, along with new datasets to address dataset limitations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current 3D anomaly segmentation research is limited, often relying on 2D post-processing techniques and lacking robust datasets for LiDAR-based tasks.

Method: The method models the feature distribution of inlier classes in feature space to identify anomalies, without relying on post-processing techniques.

Result: The proposed approach achieves state-of-the-art and competitive results on existing and newly introduced datasets for 3D LiDAR anomaly segmentation.

Conclusion: The new approach and datasets advance the field of 3D anomaly segmentation, improving upon both methods and resources available for autonomous and robotic systems.

Abstract: Understanding the surrounding environment is fundamental in autonomous driving and robotic perception. Distinguishing between known classes and previously unseen objects is crucial in real-world environments, as done in Anomaly Segmentation. However, research in the 3D field remains limited, with most existing approaches applying post-processing techniques from 2D vision. To cover this lack, we propose a new efficient approach that directly operates in the feature space, modeling the feature distribution of inlier classes to constrain anomalous samples. Moreover, the only publicly available 3D LiDAR anomaly segmentation dataset contains simple scenarios, with few anomaly instances, and exhibits a severe domain gap due to its sensor resolution. To bridge this gap, we introduce a set of mixed real-synthetic datasets for 3D LiDAR anomaly segmentation, built upon established semantic segmentation benchmarks, with multiple out-of-distribution objects and diverse, complex environments. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our approach achieves state-of-the-art and competitive results on the existing real-world dataset and the newly introduced mixed datasets, respectively, validating the effectiveness of our method and the utility of the proposed datasets. Code and datasets are available at https://simom0.github.io/lido-page/.

</details>


### [275] [CNN-ViT Fusion with Adaptive Attention Gate for Brain Tumor MRI Classification: A Hybrid Deep Learning Model](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.23137)
*Syed Ibad Hasnain,Muhammad Faris,Hafiza Syeda Yusra Tirmizi,Rabail Khowaja,Hafsa Israr*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper proposes a hybrid architecture combining CNNs and Vision Transformers for improved brain tumor classification in MRI images.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Early detection and classification of brain tumors in MRI images is important yet challenging, requiring effective integration of local and global features.

Method: A hybrid model combines a SqueezeNet-style CNN for local features and MobileViT-style Transformers for global features using an Adaptive Attention Gate for context-sensitive weighting.

Result: The model achieved a test accuracy of 97.60%, an F1-score of 97.40, and a macro-average AUC of 0.9946, outperforming competitive methods on the Kaggle Brain Tumor MRI Dataset.

Conclusion: Dynamic feature weighting through the hybrid architecture improves accuracy and effectiveness in medical image classification.

Abstract: Early detection and classifying brain tumors using Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI) images is highly important but difficult to extract in medical images. Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) are good at capturing both local texture and spatial information whereas Vision Transformers (ViTs) are good at capturing long-range global dependencies. We propose a new hybrid architecture that combines a SqueezeNet-style CNN branch with a MobileViT-style global transformer branch, through an Adaptive Attention Gate mechanism, in this paper. The gate learns dynamically per-sample, per-feature weights to weight the contribution of each branch, allowing context-sensitive merging of local and global representations. The proposed model has a test accuracy of 97.60, a precision of 97.30, a recall of 97.50, an F1-score of 97.40, and a macro-average area under the curve (AUC) of 0.9946 with a trained and evaluated on the Brain Tumor MRI Dataset (Kaggle). These scores are higher than single CNN and ViT baselines, and current competitive fusion methods, showing that dynamic feature weighting is an effective way to classify medical images.

</details>


### [276] [ARETE: Attention-based Rasterized Encoding for Topology Estimation using HSV-transformed Crowdsourced Vehicle Fleet Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.24353)
*Daniel Fritz,Dimitrios Lagamtzis,Michael Mink,Markus Enzweiler,Steffen Schober*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper proposes a novel method to generate High-Definition (HD) maps using crowdsourced vehicle trajectories, employing a Detection Transformer (DETR)-based approach.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: As autonomous driving evolves, HD maps need to be continually updated with accurate representations of road topology and lane-level features, a difficult task when relying on traditional methods. Crowdsourced data presents a scalable solution for this.

Method: The authors use a DETR-based approach where vehicle trajectories are converted into rasterized representations to predict vectorized lane attributes. The process includes dividing the map into tiles, aggregating trajectory data, and encoding it to predict directional lanes and lane dividers constrained by centerlines.

Result: The method is tested on an internal dataset and public datasets (nuScenes and nuPlan), demonstrating its capability to generate vectorized lanes and lane attributes from crowdsourced data.

Conclusion: The proposed approach provides a scalable and efficient means of updating HD maps for autonomous driving systems by leveraging crowdsourced data and advanced machine learning techniques.

Abstract: The continuous advancement of autonomous driving (AD) introduces challenges across multiple disciplines to ensure safe and efficient driving. One such challenge is the generation of High-Definition (HD) maps, which must remain up to date and highly accurate for downstream automotive tasks. One promising approach is the use of crowdsourced data from a vehicle fleet, representing road topology and lane-level features. This work focuses on the generation of centerlines and lane dividers from crowdsourced vehicle trajectories. We adopt a Detection Transformer (DETR)-based approach, where a rasterized representation of vehicle trajectories is used as input to predict vectorized lane representations. Each lane consists of a centerline with an associated direction and corresponding lane dividers that are geometrically constrained by the centerline. Our method includes the extraction of local tiles, from which crowdsourced vehicle trajectories are aggregated. Each tile undergoes a transformation into a rasterized representation encoding both the presence and direction of each trajectory, enabling the prediction of vectorized directed lanes. Experiments are conducted on an internal dataset as well as on the public datasets nuScenes and nuPlan.

</details>


### [277] [UpstreamQA: A Modular Framework for Explicit Reasoning on Video Question Answering Tasks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.23145)
*Jason Nguyen,Ameet Rao,Alexander Chang,Ishaan Kumar,Erin Tan*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: UpstreamQA introduces a framework integrating explicit reasoning into Video Question Answering (VideoQA), improving interpretability and performance under specific conditions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the opacity of decision processes in VideoQA and enable explicit reasoning that improves both interpretability and accuracy.

Method: A modular approach that separates reasoning into upstream modules for object identification and scene context generation, using multimodal LRMs and passing results to LMMs for VideoQA.

Result: Explicit reasoning improves VideoQA performance and interpretability on OpenEQA and NExTQA datasets, though it can degrade performance when baseline accuracy is already high.

Conclusion: UpstreamQA advances VideoQA by combining explicit reasoning and multimodal understanding, though its benefits depend on baseline model performance.

Abstract: Video Question Answering (VideoQA) demands models that jointly reason over spatial, temporal, and linguistic cues. However, the task's inherent complexity often requires multi-step reasoning that current large multimodal models (LMMs) perform implicitly, leaving their internal decision process opaque. In contrast, large reasoning models (LRMs) explicitly generate intermediate logical steps that enhance interpretability and can improve multi-hop reasoning accuracy. Yet, these models are not designed for native video understanding, as they typically rely on static frame sampling. We propose UpstreamQA, a modular framework that disentangles and evaluates core video reasoning components through explicit upstream reasoning modules. Specifically, we employ multimodal LRMs to perform object identification and scene context generation before passing enriched reasoning traces to downstream LMMs for VideoQA. We evaluate UpstreamQA on the OpenEQA and NExTQA datasets using two LRMs (o4-mini, Gemini 2.5 Pro) and two LMMs (GPT-4o, Gemini 2.5 Flash). Our results demonstrate that introducing explicit reasoning can significantly boost performance and interpretability of downstream VideoQA, but can also lead to performance degradation when baseline performance is sufficiently high. Overall, UpstreamQA offers a principled framework for combining explicit reasoning and multimodal understanding, advancing both performance and diagnostic transparency in VideoQA in several scenarios.

</details>


### [278] [BSViT: A Burst Spiking Vision Transformer for Expressive and Efficient Visual Representation Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.23165)
*Hongxiang Peng,Dewei Bai,Hong Qu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper presents BSViT, a burst spiking-driven Vision Transformer that resolves limitations of binary spike coding and global self-attention, achieving high accuracy and energy efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome the limitations of restricted information capacity in binary spike coding and computational overhead in dense token interactions within existing spiking vision transformers.

Method: The study introduces BSViT with a Dual-Channel Burst Spiking Self-Attention mechanism, burst spike coding for enhanced representation, and a patch adjacency masking strategy to reduce spike activity and computational costs.

Result: BSViT shows superior performance in accuracy while maintaining competitive energy efficiency on both static and event-based vision tasks.

Conclusion: The approach improves spiking transformation systems in visual tasks by addressing coding limitations and computational efficiency, contributing to more practical implementations in neuromorphic computing.

Abstract: Spiking Vision Transformers (S-ViTs) offer a promising framework for energy-efficient visual learning. However, existing designs remain limited by two fundamental issues: the restricted information capacity of binary spike coding and the dense token interactions introduced by global self-attention. To address these challenges, this work proposes BSViT, a burst spiking-driven Vision Transformer featuring a Dual-Channel Burst Spiking Self-Attention (DBSSA) mechanism. DBSSA encodes queries with binary spikes and keys with burst spikes to enhance representational capacity. The value pathway adopts dual excitatory and inhibitory binary channels, enabling signed modulation and richer spike interactions. Importantly, the entire attention operation preserves addition-only computation, ensuring compatibility with energy-efficient neuromorphic hardware. To further reduce spike activity and incorporate spatial priors, a patch adjacency masking strategy is introduced to restrict attention to local neighborhoods, resulting in structure-aware sparsity and reduced computational overhead. In addition, burst spike coding is systematically integrated across the network to increase spike-level representational capacity beyond conventional binary spiking. Extensive experiments on both static and event-based vision benchmarks demonstrate that BSViT consistently outperforms existing spiking Transformers in accuracy while maintaining competitive energy efficiency.

</details>


### [279] [A Topology fixated Shape Gradient Framework for Non Simple Boundary Extraction for CIE Lab color images with Repulsive Energy](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.23167)
*Shafeequdheen Palengara,Jyotiranjan Nayak,Vijayakrishna Rowthu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper presents a hybrid segmentation method that avoids level sets and uses a modified shape gradient approach. It achieves effective image segmentation while preventing boundary self-intersections.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address challenges in segmenting images with disjoint regions, multiple boundaries, and nested structures while preventing boundary self-intersection during evolution.

Method: The approach uses a non-local shape-based energy and discrete curve evolution, incorporating a multivariable function based on sampled curve points to handle self-intersections.

Result: The method was successfully applied to grayscale, color, nested structure images, and astronomical scenarios, achieving effective and controlled segmentation results.

Conclusion: The segmentation algorithm demonstrates robust performance in handling complex image scenarios while ensuring topological control and preventing boundary self-intersections.

Abstract: A levelset free but a hybrid image segmentation approach based on a modified version of the piece wise constant shape gradient of an Mumford Shah shape functional and a repulsive function is considered. The segmentation is performed a non-local shape based through an evolution of discrete curves driven by a non local shape based energy to segment images containing disjoint regions and multiple boundaries. This formulation has a novel additional component as a multivariable function dependent on a few sampled points of the curves that handles the occurrence of self intersection during boundary curves evolution. The method is applied to a few gray scale and color images, including images with nested structures and astronomical objects. The results indicate effective segmentation in complex scenarios with absolute control on the topology of the segments and self-intersections of the boundaries

</details>


### [280] [One Identity, Many Roles: Multimodal Entity Coreference for Enhanced Video Situation Recognition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.23173)
*Balaji Darur,Amanmeet Garg,Makarand Tapaswi*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper introduces the CineMEC framework for Video Situation Recognition, which aims to understand who did what to whom, with what, how, and where in videos. It improves both visual grounding and captioning outcomes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to address the need for coherent video understanding by ensuring consistent entity identification across both textual descriptions and visual appearances in videos.

Method: The paper proposes a Multimodal Entity Coreference (MEC) approach. CineMEC uses a multi-stage pipeline to link event role descriptions with visual clusters of entities without requiring explicit grounding supervision during training.

Result: CineMEC improves video captioning by +2.5% CIDEr and +7% LEA, and visual grounding accuracy by +18% HOTA on the extended VidSitu dataset.

Conclusion: The proposed CineMEC framework demonstrates how advancing either visual grounding or captioning consistency results in improvements to the other, achieving state-of-the-art performance for Video Situation Recognition.

Abstract: Video Situation Recognition (VidSitu) addresses the challenging problem of "who did what to whom, with what, how, and where" in a video. It tests thorough video understanding by requiring identification of salient actions and associated short descriptions for event roles across multiple events. Grounding with VidSitu requires spatio-temporal localization of key entities across shots and varied appearances.
  We posit that coherent video understanding requires consistent identification of entities that play different roles. We propose Multimodal Entity Coreference (MEC) to unite entity descriptions in text with grounding across the video. Towards this, we introduce CineMEC, a multi-stage approach that unites event role mention groups with visual clusters of entities, without explicit grounding supervision during training. Our approach is designed to exploit the synergy between visual grounding and captioning, where improving one influences the other and vice versa. For evaluation, we extend the VidSitu dataset with grounding annotations. While previous work focuses primarily on descriptions, CineMEC improves consistency across both: captioning (+2.5% CIDEr, +7% LEA) and visual grounding (+18% HOTA).

</details>


### [281] [DyABD: The Abdominal Muscle Segmentation in Dynamic MRI Benchmark](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.23187)
*Niamh Belton,Victoria Joppin,Aonghus Lawlor,Catherine Masson,Thierry Bege,David Bendahan,Kathleen M. Curran*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper introduces DyABD, a novel dynamic abdominal MRI benchmark dataset, focusing on muscle segmentation, and evaluates current segmentation models, highlighting areas for improvement.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The study aims to address the complexities of medical image segmentation for abdominal hernias, a clinically significant area with high recurrence, by establishing a challenging dataset.

Method: A new dataset, DyABD, featuring dynamic MRIs with extensive variability, pre- and post-corrective scans, and muscle annotations, was created. Current segmentation models were evaluated on this dataset under supervised, few-shot, and zero-shot paradigms.

Result: Segmentation models achieved a Dice Coefficient of 0.82, indicating potential for substantial progress in medical image segmentation.

Conclusion: DyABD redefines benchmarks for segmentation model generalization and underscores the need for advancements in medical image segmentation.

Abstract: This work introduces DyABD, a novel and complex benchmark dataset of dynamic abdominal MRIs from patients with abdominal hernias and associated high quality abdominal muscle annotations. DyABD is the first-of-its-kind in four key ways; (1) it proposes the first abdominal muscle segmentation task, (2) the dynamic MRIs are acquired whilst the patients perform various exercises, introducing extreme anatomical variability, making it one of the most challenging segmentation datasets to date, (3) it includes both pre and post corrective MRIs and (4) DyABD promotes clinical research into the high recurrence rates of abdominal hernias. Beyond dataset introduction, this work provides a comprehensive evaluation of the generalisation capabilities of existing segmentation models across Supervised, Few Shot and Zero Shot paradigms on the unseen DyABD dataset. This work reveals that there is still room for substantial improvement in the field of medical image segmentation, with the majority of techniques achieving a Dice Coefficient of 0.82. This work therefore sheds light on the true progress of the field and redefines the benchmark for progress in medical image segmentation.

</details>


### [282] [AnalogRetriever: Learning Cross-Modal Representations for Analog Circuit Retrieval](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.23195)
*Yihan Wang,Lei Li,Yao Lai,Jing Wang,Yan Lu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper introduces AnalogRetriever, a tri-modal search framework for analog circuits, improving upon limitations of existing methods by enabling cross-modal retrieval across netlists, schematics, and functional descriptions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome challenges in analog circuit design search due to reliance on single-modality matching and lack of cross-modal semantic understanding.

Method: AnalogRetriever encodes netlists, schematics, and descriptions into a shared embedding space using a vision-language model and a relational graph-based approach, with curriculum contrastive learning.

Result: AnalogRetriever shows a 75.2% Recall@1 across six cross-modal directions and enhances functional pass rates within the AnalogCoder framework.

Conclusion: The framework significantly advances analog circuit search by enabling effective cross-modal retrieval and improving task performance. Dataset and codes will be shared publicly.

Abstract: Analog circuit design relies heavily on reusing existing intellectual property (IP), yet searching across heterogeneous representations such as SPICE netlists, schematics, and functional descriptions remains challenging. Existing methods are largely limited to exact matching within a single modality, failing to capture cross-modal semantic relationships. To bridge this gap, we present AnalogRetriever, a unified tri-modal retrieval framework for analog circuit search. We first build a high-quality dataset on top of Masala-CHAI through a two-stage repair pipeline that raises the netlist compile rate from 22\% to 100\%. Built on this foundation, AnalogRetriever encodes schematics and descriptions with a vision-language model and netlists with a port-aware relational graph convolutional network, mapping all three modalities into a shared embedding space via curriculum contrastive learning. Experiments show that AnalogRetriever achieves an average Recall@1 of 75.2\% across all six cross-modal retrieval directions, significantly outperforming existing baselines. When integrated into the AnalogCoder agentic framework as a retrieval-augmented generation module, it consistently improves functional pass rates and enables previously unsolved tasks to be completed. Our code and dataset will be released.

</details>


### [283] [Micro-Expression-Aware Avatar Fingerprinting via Inter-Frame Feature Differencing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.23247)
*Masoumeh Chapariniya,Jean-Marc Odobez,Volker Dellwo,Teodora Vuković*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper focuses on verifying the driver of synthetic talking-head videos and introduces a preprocessing-free, micro-expression-aware system that analyzes motion dynamics rather than relying on visual landmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current methods for avatar fingerprinting depend on fixed, non-differentiable landmark extraction, preventing an end-to-end optimization process. This limits performance and adaptability.

Method: The authors develop a system that avoids external preprocessing and uses a micro-expression-aware backbone. The approach processes raw video frames, employing inter-frame feature differencing in the learned deep feature space to emphasize motion dynamics while suppressing static appearance features.

Result: Experiments on the NVFAIR dataset demonstrate that the model's focus on temporal motion significantly improves discriminatory performance. The system achieves an AUC of 0.877 and outperforms landmark-based baselines in many scenarios.

Conclusion: The proposed method eliminates the dependence on preprocessing and landmarks, showing that focusing on temporal motion dynamics via an optimized backbone and differencing is key to accurate avatar fingerprinting.

Abstract: Avatar fingerprinting, i.e., verifying who drives a synthetic talking-head video rather than whether it is real, is a critical safeguard for authorized use of face-reenactment technology. Existing methods rely on a fixed, non-differentiable landmark extraction stage that prevents the fingerprinting model from being optimized end-to-end from raw pixels. We propose a preprocessing-free system built on a micro-expression-aware backbone operating on raw video frames, with inter-frame feature differencing as the core design principle: consecutive feature maps are subtracted in the learned deep feature space, so that temporally stable appearance dimensions contribute zero to the output while driver-specific motion dynamics are preserved. A controlled ablation on NVFAIR confirms that temporal motion accounts for the large majority of discriminative performance, and that raw appearance features actively degrade identity separation. Both the choice of backbone and the differencing principle are essential: differencing alone is insufficient when applied to a generic encoder, as appearance-dominated features collapse to near-identical representations across adjacent frames, while the micro-expression-aware F5C backbone retains measurable motion variation that the differencing operation can exploit. Without any external preprocessing, our model achieves an overall AUC of 0.877 on NVFAIR and matches or exceeds the landmark-based baseline on the majority of cross-generator pairs.

</details>


### [284] [MotionHiFlow: Text-to-motion via hierarchical flow matching](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.23264)
*Heng Li,Xiaotong Lin,Ling-An Zeng,Yulei Kang,Shuai Li,Jian-Fang Hu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: MotionHiFlow, a hierarchical flow matching model, improves text-to-motion generation by progressively modeling motions from coarse to fine temporal scales, achieving state-of-the-art results.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current text-to-motion methods lack semantic alignment and temporal coherence due to single temporal scale operation, prompting the need for a hierarchical approach inspired by human cognition.

Method: The proposed MotionHiFlow uses hierarchical flow paths across temporal scales for motion generation, supported by a cross-scale transition process and joint-aware positional encoding with skeletal topology.

Result: MotionHiFlow achieves state-of-the-art results on HumanML3D and KIT-ML datasets, with ablation studies validating its hierarchical design and core components.

Conclusion: The hierarchical framework effectively addresses limitations in text-to-motion generation by improving semantic alignment, temporal coherence, and fine-grained details, with validation on benchmarks.

Abstract: Text-to-motion generation aims to generate 3D human motions that are tightly aligned with the input text while remaining physically plausible and rich in fine-grained detail. Although recent approaches can produce complex and natural movements, they usually operate at only one temporal scale, which limits both semantic alignment and temporal coherence. Inspired by the fact that complex motions are conceptualized hierarchically rather than at a single temporal scale in the human cognitive system, we propose \textit{MotionHiFlow}, a hierarchical flow matching framework to generate motion progressively by constructing flow path from low to high temporal scales. The flows at lower scales capture high-level semantics and coarse motion structures, while flows at higher scales refine temporal details. To link the flows across scales, we introduce a novel cross-scale transition process, ensuring continuity and preserving noise consistency. Furthermore, by integrating a Text-Motion Diffusion Transformer and a topology-aware Motion VAE, MotionHiFlow explicitly models structural dependencies among joints via joint-aware positional encoding and skeletal topology, enabling precise semantic alignment alongside fine-grained motion details. Extensive experiments on HumanML3D and KIT-ML benchmarks demonstrate state-of-the-art performance, with ablation studies confirming the effectiveness of the hierarchical design and key components. Code is available at https://github.com/ai-lh/MotionHiFlow.

</details>


### [285] [LatentBurst: A Fast and Efficient Multi Frame Super-Resolution for Hexadeca-Bayer Pattern CIS images](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.23268)
*Sangwook Baek,Vin Van Duong,Karam Park,Pilkyu Park*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper introduces LatentBurst, a novel network for multi-frame super-resolution on burst hexadeca Bayer pattern images, addressing challenges like interpolation difficulty, motion misalignment, and mobile efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper aims to resolve challenges in reconstructing high-quality burst CIS images, including difficulties in interpolating the hexadeca-Bayer pattern, motion misalignment due to large object/camera movement, and the need for real-time efficiency on mobile devices.

Method: The paper proposes LatentBurst with the following features: pyramid align and fusion in latent features for motion handling, an efficient UNet-based structure for mobile efficiency, and fine-tuned optical flow estimation with two-step knowledge distillation to reduce domain gaps.

Result: Experimental comparisons across various scenarios show that the proposed LatentBurst outperforms other state-of-the-art methods in super-resolution reconstruction.

Conclusion: LatentBurst effectively resolves limitations in burst CIS image reconstruction, enabling improved quality and efficiency, particularly for mobile applications.

Abstract: This paper introduces a novel multi frame super-resolution network (MFSR) for burst hexadeca Bayer pattern Contact Image Sensor (CIS) images, which includes demosaicing, denoising, multi-frame fusion, and super-resolution. Designing a high-quality reconstruction network poses several challenges as follows: 1) Unlike the Bayer color filter array (CFA) pattern, it is hard to interpolate hexadeca-Bayer pattern since the pixel distance between the same color groups increases; 2) Due to large object motion and camera movements, the final fusion result usually suffers the misalignment resulting a blurry image or ghosting artifacts; 3) The proposed network should be fast and efficient enough to operate in real-time on mobile devices. To overcome these challenges, we propose a novel network, called LatentBurst, which contains: 1) a pyramid align and fusion approach in latent feature to deal with large motion scenario; 2) an efficient UNet-based structure which can run efficiently on mobile device; 3) fine-tuned optical flow estimation and two-step knowledge distillation to reduce domain-gap more effectively. Experimental results in various scenarios demonstrate the effectiveness of our proposed method compared with other state-of-the-art methods.

</details>


### [286] [A Hierarchical Ensemble Inference Pipeline for Robust White Blood Cell Classification Under Domain Shifts](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.23271)
*Ruyi Dai,Tingkwong Ng,Hao Chen*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper proposes a memory-augmented pipeline for WBC classification to tackle domain shifts, achieving a top-ten macro F1-score in the WBCBench challenge.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the challenge of domain shifts in automated white blood cell classification, which hinder real-world deployment, especially for leukemia screening.

Method: The authors introduce a hierarchical ensemble pipeline utilizing a memory-augmented feature bank, DinoBloom backbone fine-tuned with LoRA, and k-nearest neighbors (kNN) for robust classification under domain shifts.

Result: The method demonstrated robustness by ranking in the top ten based on macro F1-score in the WBCBench challenge final testing phase.

Conclusion: The proposed approach effectively mitigates domain shifts and shows promise for robust white blood cell classification, particularly for leukemia screening applications.

Abstract: Automated white blood cell (WBC) classification is essential for scalable leukaemia screening. However, real-world deployment is challenged by domain shifts caused by staining protocols, scanner characteristics, and inter-laboratory variability, which often degrade model performance. The White Blood Cell Classification Challenge (WBCBench) at ISBI 2026 aims to advance robust WBC recognition, with a focus on accurately identifying blast cells and other clinically critical rare subtypes. We propose a memory-augmented, hierarchical ensemble pipeline for WBC classification under domain shifts, leveraging a feature bank and a DinoBloom backbone fine-tuned with LoRA. Our three-stage inference hierarchy combines k-nearest neighbors (kNN) retrieval at each level, reducing over-reliance on any single decision. Evaluated on the WBCBench dataset, our method ranks within the top ten by macro F1-score in the final testing phase.

</details>


### [287] [SemiGDA: Generative Dual-distribution Alignment for Semi-Supervised Medical Image Segmentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.23274)
*Kaiwen Huang,Yi Zhou,Yizhe Zhang,Jingxiong Li,Tao Zhou*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper introduces SemiGDA, a new semi-supervised framework for medical image segmentation using generative dual-distribution alignment, enhancing feature and semantic distributions to improve performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the limitations of traditional segmentation methods that rely on labeled data, undermining robust semantic representation and adaptability to unlabeled data in medical image analysis.

Method: Propose a Dual-distribution Alignment Module (DAM) for aligning image and mask feature distributions via latent space constraints, combined with a Consistency-Driven Skip Adapter (CDSA) for multi-scale feature fusion and semantic alignment.

Result: Achieved superior performance in medical image segmentation compared to state-of-the-art semi-supervised methods when tested on diverse datasets.

Conclusion: SemiGDA effectively leverages unlabeled data, aligns feature/semantic distributions, and demonstrates enhanced semantic learning, making it a promising solution for label-scarce scenarios in medical segmentation.

Abstract: Semi-supervised learning addresses label scarcity and high annotation costs in medical image segmentation by exploiting the latent information in unlabeled data to enhance model performance. Traditional discriminative segmentation relies on segmentation masks, neglecting feature-level distribution constraints. This limits robust semantic representation learning and adaptive modeling of unlabeled data in scenarios with few labels. To address these limitations, we propose SemiGDA, a novel Generative Dual-distribution Alignment framework for semi-supervised medical image segmentation. Our SemiGDA overcomes the reliance of discriminative methods on large labeled datasets by aligning feature and semantic distributions to boost semantic learning and scene adaptability. Specifically, we propose a Dual-distribution Alignment Module (DAM), which employs two structurally distinct encoders to model image and mask feature distributions. It enforces their alignment in the latent space via distributional constraints, establishing structured feature consistency. Moreover, we design a Consistency-Driven Skip Adapter (CDSA) strategy, which introduces dual skip adapters (Image and Mask) to fuse multi-scale features via skip connections. Using a consistency loss, CDSA enhances cross-branch semantic alignment and reinforces fine-grained semantic consistency. Experimental results on diverse medical datasets show that our method outperforms other state-of-the-art semi-supervised segmentation methods. Code is released at: https://github.com/taozh2017/SemiGDA.

</details>


### [288] [Lightweight and Production-Ready PDF Visual Element Parsing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.23276)
*Meizhu Liu,Yassi Abbasi,Matthew Rowe,Michael Avendi,Paul Li*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper introduces a new PDF parsing framework to address shortcomings in detecting and accurately associating visual elements, achieving high accuracy and substantial improvements in applications of multimodal retrieval-augmented generation (RAG).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing PDF parsers fail to effectively detect complex visual elements, associate captions correctly, and produce reliable outputs, leading to suboptimal results in downstream applications like document retrieval and question answering.

Method: The framework combines spatial heuristics, layout analysis, and semantic similarity to accurately detect visual elements and link captions with corresponding visuals.

Result: The system achieves ≥96% visual element detection accuracy and 93% caption association accuracy, outperforming existing solutions in multimodal RAG tasks and reducing latency significantly.

Conclusion: This lightweight, efficient framework offers a robust solution to PDF parsing challenges, improving multimodal retrieval and has been successfully adopted in production environments.

Abstract: PDF documents contain critical visual elements such as figures, tables, and forms whose accurate extraction is essential for document understanding and multimodal retrieval-augmented generation (RAG). Existing PDF parsers often miss complex visuals, extract non-informative artifacts (e.g., watermarks, logos), produce fragmented elements, and fail to reliably associate captions with their corresponding elements, which degrades downstream retrieval and question answering. We present a lightweight and production level PDF parsing framework that can accurately detect visual elements and associates captions using a combination of spatial heuristics, layout analysis, and semantic similarity. On popular benchmark datasets and internal product data, the proposed solution achieves $\geq96\%$ visual element detection accuracy and $93\%$ caption association accuracy. When used as a preprocessing step for multimodal RAG, it significantly outperforms state-of-the-art parsers and large vision-language models on both internal data and the MMDocRAG benchmark, while reducing latency by over $2\times$. We have deployed the proposed system in challenging production environment.

</details>


### [289] [Bridging the Pose-Semantic Gap: A Cascade Framework for Text-Based Person Anomaly Search](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.23282)
*Zequn Xie,Guijin Luo,Chuxin Wang,Sihang Cai,Tao Jin,Zhou Zhao,Yixuan Tang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper proposes a system called SSDC for semantic retrieval of behavioral events using decoupled structure-aware and semantic pathways, achieving state-of-the-art performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the Pose-Semantic Gap problem in text-based person anomaly search, where similar skeletal geometries may represent different actions, and improve the efficiency of large-scale semantic retrieval.

Method: The SSDC framework splits the retrieval process into two parts: (1) a Structure-Aware Coarse Retrieval to filter skeletal similarities quickly and (2) a Detective Squad Interaction module with specialized agents (Detective, Analyst, Writer) to verify and process the semantics.

Result: SSDC outperforms existing methods in accuracy and efficiency on the PAB benchmark by effectively combining structural filtering and semantic reasoning.

Conclusion: The decoupled approach of SSDC provides a scalable, efficient, and semantically robust solution for behavioral event retrieval, bridging the Pose-Semantic Gap.

Abstract: Text-based person anomaly search retrieves specific behavioral events from surveillance archives using natural-language queries. Although recent pose-aware methods align geometric structures well, they face a fundamental Pose-Semantic Gap: semantically different actions can share similar skeletal geometries. While Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) can reduce this ambiguity, using them for large-scale retrieval is computationally prohibitive. We propose the Structure-Semantic Decoupled Cascade (SSDC) framework, which decouples retrieval into two stages: (1) Structure-Aware Coarse Retrieval, where a lightweight model quickly filters candidates by skeletal similarity ; and (2) Detective Squad Interaction, a multi-agent semantic verification module. The squad consists of a Detective for fast binary filtering, an Analyst for evidence extraction, and a Writer for semantic synthesis. Finally, we re-rank candidates by fusing the synthesized captions with structural priors. Experiments on the PAB benchmark show that SSDC achieves state-of-the-art performance by balancing efficiency and semantic reasoning.

</details>


### [290] [MetaErr: Towards Predicting Error Patterns in Deep Neural Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.23289)
*Varun Totakura,Shayok Chakraborty*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper introduces MetaErr, a framework to predict failures in deep learning models by training a meta-model, independent of the base model's architecture and parameters. It demonstrates improved performance in pseudo-labeling and outperforms baselines in computer vision datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Deep learning systems occasionally fail abruptly without warning or explanation. While error rate reduction is a focus, predicting failures directly has been overlooked, which is critical for reliable multimedia applications.

Method: The researchers propose MetaErr, which trains a meta-model to observe the base model's performance in predicting data samples and determine if it will succeed or fail. The meta-model works independently of the base model's architecture and training specifics.

Result: Empirical studies suggest that MetaErr is effective compared to competing methods. It also improves pseudo-labeling-based semi-supervised learning and outperforms strong baselines across three benchmark datasets in computer vision.

Conclusion: MetaErr presents a simple yet powerful solution for predicting failures in deep neural networks, enhancing multimedia application reliability and paving the way for improved semi-supervised learning strategies.

Abstract: Due to the unprecedented success of deep learning, it has become an integral component in several multimedia computing applications in todays world. Unfortunately, deep learning systems are not perfect and can fail, sometimes abruptly, without prior warning or explanation. While reducing the error rate of deep neural networks has been the primary focus of the multimedia community, the problem of predicting when a deep learning system is going to fail has received significantly less research attention. In this paper, we propose a simple yet effective framework, MetaErr, to address this under-explored problem in deep learning research. We train a meta-model whose goal is to predict whether a base deep neural network will succeed or fail in predicting a particular data sample, by observing the base models performance on a given learning task. The meta-model is completely agnostic of the architecture and training parameters of the base model. Such an error prediction system can be immensely useful in a variety of smart multimedia applications. Our empirical studies corroborate the promise and potential of our framework against competing baselines. We further demonstrate the usefulness of our framework to improve the performance of pseudo-labeling-based semi-supervised learning, and show that MetaErr outperforms several strong baselines on three benchmark computer vision datasets.

</details>


### [291] [STAND: Semantic Anchoring Constraint with Dual-Granularity Disambiguation for Remote Sensing Image Change Captioning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.23309)
*Yanpei Gong,Beichen Zhang,Hao Wang,Zhaobo Qi,Xinyan Liu,Yuanrong Xu,Ruiyang Gao,Weigang Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper introduces STAND, a method for remote sensing image change captioning (RSICC) that addresses ambiguities in viewpoint, scale, and prior knowledge.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To deal with challenges of ambiguities in viewpoint, scale, and prior knowledge for effectively capturing changes in remote sensing image descriptions.

Method: The STAND framework includes an interpretable constraint for regularizing temporal representations, a dual-granularity disambiguation module for spatial uncertainties, and a semantic concept anchoring module for accurate textual transformation.

Result: The proposed method demonstrates superior performance and effectively resolves the inherent ambiguities in RSICC tasks.

Conclusion: STAND improves RSICC by integrating semantic anchoring and disambiguation strategies, offering reliable and precise change captions.

Abstract: Remote sensing image change captioning (RSICC) aims to describe the difference between two remote sensing images. While recent methods have explored video modeling, they largely overlook the inherent ambiguities in viewpoint, scale, and prior knowledge, lacking effective constraints on the encoder. In this paper, we present STAND, a Semantic Anchoring Constraint with Dual-Granularity Disambiguation for RSICC, to progressively resolve these ambiguities. Specifically, to establish a reliable feature foundation, we first introduce an interpretable constraint to regularize temporal representations. Operating on these purified features, a dual-granularity disambiguation module resolves spatial uncertainties by coupling macro-level global context aggregation for viewpoint confusion with micro-level frequency-refocused attention for small-object scale enhancement. Ultimately, to translate these visually disambiguated features into precise text, a semantic concept anchoring module leverages language categorical priors to tackle knowledge ambiguity during decoding. Extensive experiments verify the superiority of STAND and its effectiveness in addressing ambiguities.

</details>


### [292] [Learning from Noisy Prompts: Saliency-Guided Prompt Distillation for Robust Segmentation with SAM](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.23314)
*Jingxuan Kang,Ziqi Zhang,Shaoming Zheng,Shuang Li,Uday Bharat Patel,Alexander Harry Fitzhugh,Phillip Lung,Yusuf Kiberu,Nikesh Jathanna,Shahnaz Jamil-Copley,Bernhard Kainz,Chen Qin*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper introduces the SPD framework to enhance the Segment Anything Model (SAM) for reliable medical imaging segmentation despite imprecise prompts.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the unreliability of SAM when faced with weak, generic, and noisy prompts common in clinical workflows.

Method: SPD uses a lightweight saliency head to generate localization maps, contextual prompt distillation to refine prompts, and a Pairwise Slice Consistency objective to enforce anatomical coherence.

Result: Experiments on MRI and CT datasets show SPD outperforms existing methods, achieving significant improvements in segmentation metrics.

Conclusion: SPD enables precise segmentation in clinical environments, offering a robust solution for foundation model deployment despite imperfect prompts.

Abstract: Segmentation is central to clinical diagnosis and monitoring, yet the reliability of modern foundation models in medical imaging still depends on the availability of precise prompts. The Segment Anything Model (SAM) offers powerful zero-shot capabilities, although it collapses under the weak, generic, and noisy prompts that dominate real clinical workflows. In practice, annotations such as centerline points are coarse and ambiguous, often drifting across neighboring anatomy and misguiding SAM toward inconsistent or incomplete masks. We introduce SPD, a Saliency-Guided Prompt Distillation framework that converts these unreliable cues into robust guidance. SPD first learns data-driven anatomical priors through a lightweight saliency head to obtain confident localization maps. These priors then drive Contextual Prompt Distillation, which validates and enriches noisy prompts using cues from anatomically adjacent slices, producing a consensus prompt set that matches the behavior of expert reasoning. A Pairwise Slice Consistency objective further enforces local anatomical coherence during segmentation.
