Motivation: Selecting suitable cloud hardware for HPC jobs is traditionally manual, expertise-intensive, and error-prone, requiring users to account for evolving cloud options and workload-specific constraints.

Method: The paper introduces Incisor, a system combining program analysis and large language model (LLM)-guided reasoning to infer hardware requirements solely from submission artifacts (e.g., executable, inputs).

Result: Incisor achieves 100% success in selecting working AWS EC2 instances for first-time runs and reduces runtime by 54% and instance costs by 44% against expert-derived baselines.

Conclusion: Incisor automates and optimizes the cloud instance selection process, demonstrating its efficacy in reducing costs and runtime while maintaining high job success rates.

Abstract: We present Incisor, a cloud HPC job submission system for the ex ante instance selection problem: choosing suitable hardware in the challenging but common setting where only the executable, inputs, and invocation commands are available at submission time. In practice, this task is manual and expertise-intensive, requiring users to combine incomplete knowledge of rapidly evolving cloud offerings with workload-specific intuition, static analysis, and systems reasoning to infer hardware constraints and select an instance type for each job. Incisor automates this process by pairing widely available program analysis tools with LLM-guided reasoning to infer hardware requirements and choose cloud instances. Using submission artifacts alone, Incisor atop frontier coding LLMs selects working AWS EC2 instances ex ante for 100% of first-time runs of source-compiled (C, C++, Fortran) and Python applications. Against a strong baseline combining expert-derived constraints with SkyPilot's instance selection, Incisor cuts job runtime by 54% and instance costs by 44%.

</details>


### [436] [SpotVista: Availability-Aware Recommendation System for Reliable and Cost-Efficient Multi-Node Spot Instances](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.24548)
*Taeyoon Kim,Kyumin Kim,Kyunghwan Kim,Hayoung Kim,Seungwoo Jeong,Moohyun Song,Kyungyong Lee*

Main category: cs.DC

TL;DR: SpotVista is introduced as a system to optimize the reliability and cost-efficiency of multi-node spot instances in cloud environments, surpassing existing solutions by leveraging novel datasets and methodologies.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Cloud spot instances are cheaper but prone to interruptions, making optimizing both cost and reliability of multi-node systems crucial for modern workloads, especially as policy changes from vendors reduce the efficacy of traditional prediction data.

Method: SpotVista builds a large-scale multi-node availability dataset, addresses query limitations, and recommends configurations for reliable and cost-efficient multi-node setups using public data.

Result: SpotVista demonstrated superior performance over existing tools, achieving 81.28% higher availability and 2.84% more cost savings versus SpotVerse, and 21.6% greater stability and 26.3% more cost savings compared to AWS SpotFleet.

Conclusion: SpotVista effectively optimizes multi-node spot instance selection, improving reliability and cost-savings for cloud systems, making it a valuable advancement over current state-of-the-art solutions.

Abstract: Cloud vendors offer discounted spot instances to maximize surplus resource utilization, but these instances are subject to the risk of sudden interruption. Traditional pricing datasets have been employed to predict this risk, yet recent policy changes by cloud vendors have diminished their effectiveness. To promote spot instance usage, public cloud vendors provide instant availability datasets to help users mitigate interruption risks. While existing research utilizing this data has proposed methods to reduce interruptions, these studies have primarily focused on single-node instances, overlooking the stability of multi-node environments widely adopted for modern cloud workloads.
  This paper proposes SpotVista, a system that recommends a resource pool of reliable and cost-efficient multi-node spot instances by leveraging various publicly available datasets. To achieve this, SpotVista collects a large-scale multi-node availability dataset while overcoming significant query limitations. Through a thorough analysis of multi-node spot instance availability behavior, SpotVista establishes a methodology for recommending cost-efficient and reliable multi-node configurations.
  To evaluate how effectively the proposed methodology reflects multi-node availability and cost efficiency, extensive real-world interruption experiments were conducted. The results demonstrate that SpotVista outperforms the state-of-the-art work, SpotVerse, achieving 81.28% greater availability and 2.84\% more cost savings in a multi-region setup. When compared to a publicly available service, AWS SpotFleet, SpotVista provides 21.6\% higher stability and 26.3% greater cost savings.

</details>


### [437] [Cloudless-Training: A Framework to Improve Efficiency of Geo-Distributed ML Training](https://arxiv.org/abs/2303.05330)
*Wenting Tan,Xiao Shi1,Cunchi Lv,Xiaofang Zhao*

Main category: cs.DC

TL;DR: The paper introduces Cloudless-Training, a serverless framework to enhance geo-distributed ML training's efficiency using elastic scheduling and specialized synchronization strategies.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Geo-distributed ML training faces challenges with inefficient resource scheduling and overhead from WAN communication, impairing performance and resource utilization.

Method: Cloudless-Training employs a two-layer architecture for scheduling, elastic resource deployment strategies, and novel synchronization methods like ASGD-GA and inter-PS model averaging.

Result: Experiments on Tencent Cloud show improved resource utilization (up to 24% cost reduction) and faster training (up to 1.7x speedup), while maintaining model correctness.

Conclusion: The framework supports efficient and correct geo-distributed ML training, leveraging cloud heterogeneity and reducing costs and training time significantly.

Abstract: Geo-distributed ML training can benefit many emerging ML scenarios (e.g., large model training, federated learning) with multi-regional cloud resources and wide area network. However, its efficiency is limited due to 2 challenges. First, efficient elastic scheduling of multi-regional cloud resources is usually missing, affecting resource utilization and performance of training. Second, training communication on WAN is still the main overhead, easily subjected to low bandwidth and high fluctuations of WAN. In this paper, we propose a framework, Cloudless-Training, to realize efficient PS-based geo-distributed ML training in 3 aspects. First, it uses a two-layer architecture with control and physical training planes to support elastic scheduling and communication for multi-regional clouds in a serverless maner.Second, it provides an elastic scheduling strategy that can deploy training workflows adaptively according to the heterogeneity of available cloud resources and distribution of pre-existing training datasets. Third, it provides 2 new synchronization strategies for training partitions among clouds, including asynchronous SGD with gradient accumulation (ASGD-GA) and inter-PS model averaging (MA). It is implemented with OpenFaaS and evaluated on Tencent Cloud. Experiments show that Cloudless-Training can support general ML training in a geo-distributed way, greatly improve resource utilization (e.g., 9.2%-24.0% training cost reduction) and synchronization efficiency (e.g., 1.7x training speedup over baseline at most) with model correctness guarantees.

</details>


<div id='cs.LG'></div>

# cs.LG [[Back]](#toc)

### [438] [Approximating Uniform Random Rotations by Two-Block Structured Hadamard Rotations in High Dimensions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.23418)
*Tomer Zilca,Gal Mendelson*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper investigates the approximation quality of two-block structured Hadamard rotations as substitutes for uniform random rotations. It establishes positive results for one-dimensional uniform convergence and negative results regarding high-dimensional discrepancies.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Uniform random rotations are valuable for various computations, but their generation and application in high dimensions are computationally expensive. Structured random rotations, like Hadamard transforms, are practical alternatives, but their theoretical properties remain underexplored.

Method: The study theoretically analyzes two-block structured Hadamard rotations, proving explicit bounds for one-dimensional uniform convergence and lower bounds on Wasserstein distances for full vector distributions.

Result: The results show convergence in one-dimensional approximation with a Kolmogorov-distance bound, but non-vanishing discrepancies in high-dimensional global behavior.

Conclusion: Structured Hadamard rotations partially explain their empirical success but should not uniformly substitute true random rotations due to their high-dimensional limitations.

Abstract: Uniform random rotations are a useful primitive in applications such as fast Johnson-Lindenstrauss embeddings, kernel approximation, communication-efficient learning, and recent AI compression pipelines, but they are computationally expensive to generate and apply in high dimensions. A common practical replacement is repeated structured random rotations built from Walsh-Hadamard transforms and random sign diagonals.
  Applying the structured random rotation twice has been shown empirically to be useful, but the supporting theory is still limited. In this paper we study the approximation quality achieved when using this two-block structured Hadamard rotation. Our results are both positive and negative. On the positive side, we prove that every fixed coordinate of the two-block transform converges uniformly, over all inputs, to the corresponding coordinate of a uniformly rotated vector, with an explicit Kolmogorov-distance bound of order $d^{-1/5}$. On the negative side, we prove an explicit lower bound on the Wasserstein distance between the full vector distributions, showing that the two-block transform is not a globally accurate surrogate for a uniform random rotation in the worst case. For the extremal input used in the lower bound, we also prove a matching asymptotic upper bound, showing that the lower-bound scale is sharp for that input.
  Taken together, the results identify a clear separation between one-dimensional marginal behavior, where approximation improves with dimension, and full high-dimensional geometry, where a nonvanishing discrepancy remains. This provides a partial theoretical explanation for the empirical success of structured Hadamard rotations in some algorithms, while also clarifying the limitations of treating them as drop-in replacements for true uniform random rotations.

</details>


### [439] [Collocation-based Robust Physics Informed Neural Networks for time-dependent simulations of pollution propagation under thermal inversion conditions on Spitsbergen](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.23003)
*Leszek Siwik,Maciej Sikora,Natalia Leszczyńska,Tomasz Maciej Ciesielski,Eirik Valseth,Manuela Bastidas Olivares,Marcin Łoś,Tomasz Służalec,Jacek Leszczyński,Maciej Paszyński*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces a Physics-Informed Neural Network framework for simulating pollution from moving sources and validates it with a case study on snowmobile traffic's environmental effects.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address challenges in accurately modeling pollution propagation, especially from moving emission sources, and to analyze the impact of phenomena like thermal inversion on air quality.

Method: A variational framework is developed for the advection-diffusion problem, ensuring mathematical robustness. A Physics-Informed Neural Network is then used, employing a loss function tied to approximation errors and a collocation-based method to accelerate training.

Result: The framework successfully analyzes pollution propagation, including thermal inversion effects, showing how it intensifies particulate matter concentration in Longyearbyen, Spitsbergen.

Conclusion: The proposed method is effective for studying pollution dynamics and demonstrates the environmental impact of thermal inversion, enabling better-informed decisions regarding air quality management.

Abstract: In this paper, we propose a Physics-Informed Neural Network framework for time-dependent simulations of pollution propagation originating from moving emission sources. We formulate a robust variational framework for the time-dependent advection-diffusion problem and establish the boundedness and inf-sup stability of the corresponding discrete weak formulation. Based on this mathematical foundation, we construct a robust loss function that is directly related to the true approximation error, defined as the difference between the neural network approximation and the (unknown) exact solution. Additionally, a collocation-based strategy is introduced to speed up neural network training. As a case study, we investigate pollution propagation caused by snowmobile traffic in Longyearbyen, Spitsbergen, supported by detailed in-field measurements collected using dedicated sensors. The proposed framework is applied to analyze the effects of thermal inversion on pollutant accumulation. Our results demonstrate that thermal inversion traps dense and humid air masses near the ground, significantly enhancing particulate matter (PM) concentration and worsening local air quality.

</details>


### [440] [The Spectral Lifecycle of Transformer Training: Transient Compression Waves, Persistent Spectral Gradients, and the Q/K--V Asymmetry](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.22778)
*Yi Liu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper studies the singular value spectra of transformer weight matrices during pretraining, uncovering phenomena like compression waves, depth gradients, and query/key-functional asymmetry, along with their implications.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Understanding the internal dynamics of transformers during training, particularly weight matrix behavior, and its implications on model performance and optimization.

Method: The researchers tracked singular value decomposition of transformer weight matrices across multiple steps, analyzing the training dynamics at different scales and layers of model depth.

Result: They discovered transient compression waves, persistent spectral gradients, and Q/K--V functional asymmetry during training. Further, they derived scaling laws and validated them across multiple models and parameter sizes.

Conclusion: Spectral properties such as rank and shape provide unique insights into model training dynamics, layer importance, and enable more effective pruning strategies compared to existing heuristics.

Abstract: We present the first systematic study of weight matrix singular value spectra \emph{during} transformer pretraining, tracking full SVD decompositions of every weight matrix at 25-step intervals across three model scales (30M--285M parameters). We discover three phenomena: \textbf{(1)~Transient Compression Waves:} stable rank compression propagates as a traveling wave from early to late layers, creating a dramatic gradient that peaks early then \emph{reverses} -- late layers eventually over-compress past early layers. \textbf{(2)~Persistent Spectral Gradients:} the power-law exponent~$α$ develops a permanent depth gradient forming a non-monotonic inverted-U in deeper models, with peaks shifting toward earlier layers as depth increases. \textbf{(3)~Q/K--V Functional Asymmetry:} value/output projections compress uniformly while query/key projections carry the full depth-dependent dynamics. The dissociation between transient compression and persistent spectral shape reveals that \emph{rank and spectral shape encode fundamentally different information about training}. We formalize this as a two-timescale dynamical model and derive scaling laws ($Δα\propto L^{0.26}$, $R^2{=}0.99$). We validate on nine models across three families (custom, GPT-2, Pythia; 30M--1B parameters; 8--36 layers), demonstrate that $α$ predicts layer importance ($ρ{=}0.69$--$0.84$, $p{<}0.02$), and show that spectral-guided pruning outperforms Last-N heuristics by $1.1{\times}$--$3.6{\times}$ across seven models in two families (GPT-2 124M--774M, Pythia 160M--1B), with worst-vs-best gaps up to $23.7{\times}$ confirming the causal role of spectral structure.

</details>


### [441] [Necessary and sufficient conditions for universality of Kolmogorov-Arnold networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.23765)
*Vugar Ismailov*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper investigates the universal approximation capabilities of Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks (KANs) by studying their edge functions. It proves that universality can be achieved with only one non-affine function, and explores conditions for different network depths and function types.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The study aims to understand the minimal requirements for achieving the universal approximation property in Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks (KANs), particularly focusing on the type and nature of edge functions used.

Method: The researchers analyze and prove theoretical results regarding the conditions under which KANs achieve universality. They explore the role of non-affine and affine edge functions, network depth, and specific parameterizations like splines.

Result: The main results include the finding that one non-affine function suffices for universality in deep KANs, universality conditions for two-layer networks, and the ability to use finite families of affine functions. Networks with spline-based edge parameterizations are also shown to be universal approximators.

Conclusion: Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks can achieve universality with minimal requirements, such as just one non-affine edge function, or a finite set of specific affine functions, making them efficient and flexible. These findings extend to parameterized splines.

Abstract: We analyze the universal approximation property of Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks (KANs) in terms of their edge functions. If these functions are all affine, then universality clearly fails. How many non-affine functions are needed, in addition to affine ones, to ensure universality? We show that a single one suffices. More precisely, we prove that deep KANs in which all edge functions are either affine or equal to a fixed continuous function $σ$ are dense in $C(K)$ for every compact set $K\subset\mathbb{R}^n$ if and only if $σ$ is non-affine. In contrast, for KANs with exactly two hidden layers, universality holds if and only if $σ$ is nonpolynomial. We further show that the full class of affine functions is not required; it can be replaced by a finite set without affecting universality. In particular, in the nonpolynomial case, a fixed family of five affine functions suffices when the depth is arbitrary. More generally, for every continuous non-affine function $σ$, there exists a finite affine family $A_σ$ such that deep KANs with edge functions in $A_σ\cup\{σ\}$ remain universal. We also prove that KANs with the spline-based edge parameterization introduced by Liu et al.~\cite{Liu2024} are universal approximators in the classical sense, even when the spline degree and knot sequence are fixed in advance.

</details>


### [442] [KARL: Mitigating Hallucinations in LLMs via Knowledge-Boundary-Aware Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.22779)
*Cheng Gao,Cheng Huang,Kangyang Luo,Ziqing Qiao,Shuzheng Si,Huimin Chen,Chaojun Xiao,Maosong Sun*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces KARL, a framework to help large language models (LLMs) abstain appropriately when confronted with questions beyond their knowledge, addressing hallucination issues.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To mitigate hallucinations in large language models by allowing them to abstain appropriately from responding when uncertain.

Method: Proposes KARL, which incorporates a Knowledge-Boundary-Aware Reward mechanism and a Two-Stage RL Training Strategy to adaptively align abstention behavior with models' evolving knowledge boundaries.

Result: KARL enhances the accuracy-hallucination trade-off, maintaining high accuracy while effectively reducing incorrect answers or hallucinations across diverse benchmark tests.

Conclusion: By dynamically estimating knowledge boundaries and refining abstention behavior, KARL presents a robust solution for reinforcing responsible answering in LLMs, ensuring both accuracy and reliability.

Abstract: Enabling large language models (LLMs) to appropriately abstain from answering questions beyond their knowledge is crucial for mitigating hallucinations. While existing reinforcement learning methods foster autonomous abstention, they often compromise answer accuracy because their static reward mechanisms, agnostic to models' knowledge boundaries, drive models toward excessive caution. In this work, we propose KARL, a novel framework that continuously aligns an LLM's abstention behavior with its evolving knowledge boundary. KARL introduces two core innovations: a Knowledge-Boundary-Aware Reward that performs online knowledge boundary estimation using within-group response statistics, dynamically rewarding correct answers or guided abstention; and a Two-Stage RL Training Strategy that first explores the knowledge boundary and bypasses the "abstention trap", and subsequently converts incorrect answers beyond the knowledge boundary into abstentions without sacrificing accuracy. Extensive experiments on multiple benchmarks demonstrate that KARL achieves a superior accuracy-hallucination trade-off, effectively suppressing hallucinations while maintaining high accuracy across both in-distribution and out-of-distribution scenarios.

</details>


### [443] [BiTA: Bidirectional Gated Recurrent Unit-Transformer Aggregator in a Temporal Graph Network Framework for Alert Prediction in Computer Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.22781)
*Zahra Makki Nayeri,Mohsen Rezvani*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces BiTA, a novel temporal aggregation strategy in TGNs for improved cyber threat prediction, achieving superior performance compared to state-of-the-art models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Proactively predicting alerts in computer networks is crucial for mitigating cyber threats. Existing TGNs lack robust mechanisms to capture multi-scale temporal patterns, limiting effectiveness in modeling complex real-world attack behaviors.

Method: BiTA redesigns temporal aggregation in TGNs by jointly encoding bidirectional and long-range contextual relations, enhancing temporal reasoning without altering the original TGN structure.

Result: BiTA demonstrates significant gains in performance metrics across real-world datasets, outperforming state-of-the-art models in both transductive and inductive tasks.

Conclusion: BiTA is a scalable, interpretable framework enhancing real-time cyber threat anticipation and intrusion detection systems through improved temporal graph learning.

Abstract: Proactive alert prediction in computer networks is critical for mitigating evolving cyber threats and enabling timely defensive actions. Temporal Graph Neural Networks (TGNs) provide a principled framework for modeling time-evolving interactions; however, existing TGN-based methods predominantly rely on unidirectional or single-mechanism temporal aggregation, which limits their ability to capture recursive, multi-scale temporal patterns commonly observed in real-world attack behaviors. In this paper, we propose BiTA, a Bidirectional Gated Recurrent Unit-Transformer Aggregator for temporal graph learning. Rather than introducing a deeper or higher-capacity model, BiTA redesigns the temporal aggregation function within the TGN framework by jointly encoding bidirectional sequential dependencies and long-range contextual relations over each node's temporal neighborhood. This aggregation strategy enables complementary temporal reasoning at different scales while preserving the original TGN memory and message-passing structure. We evaluate BiTA on real-world alert datasets, demonstrating significant improvements in key performance metrics such as area under the curve, average precision, mean reciprocal rank, and per-category prediction accuracy when compared to state-of-the-art temporal graph models. BiTA outperforms baseline methods under both transductive and inductive settings, highlighting its robustness and generalization capabilities in dynamic network environments. BiTA is a scalable and interpretable framework for real-time cyber threat anticipation, paving the way toward more intelligent and adaptive intrusion detection systems.

</details>


### [444] [Stochastic KV Routing: Enabling Adaptive Depth-Wise Cache Sharing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.22782)
*Anastasiia Filippova,David Grangier,Marco Cuturi,João Monteiro*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper presents an optimization approach to reduce memory requirements for caching Key-Values (KVs) in transformer model deployment, leveraging cross-layer cache sharing.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current KV caching for transformer models during autoregressive generation is memory-intensive, and existing solutions mostly focus on temporal compression or eviction, leaving depth-wise cache sharing underexplored.

Method: The authors propose a training method called "random cross-layer attention," where layers randomly attend either their own KV states or those of a preceding layer. This adapts the model for depth-wise cache sharing strategies.

Result: The proposed technique facilitates effective depth-wise cache sharing, maintains or improves performance (especially for larger models in limited data settings), and significantly reduces memory requirements for KV caching.

Conclusion: Random cross-layer attention offers a practical and efficient way to optimize KV caching, making transformer models more deployable under hardware constraints with reduced memory footprints.

Abstract: Serving transformer language models with high throughput requires caching Key-Values (KVs) to avoid redundant computation during autoregressive generation. The memory footprint of KV caching is significant and heavily impacts serving costs. This work proposes to lessen these memory requirements. While recent work has largely addressed KV cache reduction via compression and eviction along the temporal axis, we argue that the \emph{depth} dimension offers an orthogonal and robust avenue for optimization. Although prior research suggests that a full cache for every layer is redundant, implementing cross-layer cache sharing remains a practical challenge; existing methods typically suffer from reduced throughput or increased time-to-first-token. In this paper, we demonstrate that dropping a layer's cache offers efficient optimization without information loss. We propose a simple training approach: random cross-layer attention. During training, layers randomly choose to attend either to their own KV states or those of a preceding layer. This stochastic process adapts the model to be robust to various depth-wise cache sharing strategies, ensuring flexibility for unknown hardware constraints at deployment time. Our evaluations show that applying this scheme during pre-training or fine-tuning enables depth-wise cache sharing for various model families. Furthermore, for larger models in data-constrained settings, this approach is suggestive of a regularization-like effect, frequently preserving or improving performance while significantly reducing the cache's memory footprint.

</details>


### [445] [Parameter Efficiency Is Not Memory Efficiency: Rethinking Fine-Tuning for On-Device LLM Adaptation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.22783)
*Irene Tenison,Stella Ahn,Miriam Kim,Ebtisam Alshehri,Lalana Kagal*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces LARS, a memory-efficient framework for fine-tuning large language models by addressing constraints in memory usage during training.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to overcome the limitations of Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning (PEFT) methods like LoRA, which are not memory-efficient on resource-constrained or edge devices.

Method: LARS applies low-rank constraints to activation subspaces rather than model parameters, reducing memory consumption by targeting the dominant source of memory growth.

Result: LARS achieves significant memory reductions of 33.54% on GPUs and 51.95% on CPUs compared to LoRA, while maintaining accuracy and computational throughput.

Conclusion: LARS enables scalable, memory-efficient, and on-device adaptability of large language models for resource-constrained hardware, addressing a critical gap in existing PEFT techniques.

Abstract: Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning (PEFT) has become the standard for adapting large language models (LLMs). In this work we challenge the wide-spread assumption that parameter efficiency equates memory efficiency and on-device adaptability. We show that this is not true - while methods like LoRA and IA3 significantly reduce trainable parameters, they remain bound by intermediate tensors that scale linearly with sequence length, often triggering out-of-memory errors on-device. In this work, we introduce LARS (Low-memory Activation-Rank Subspace), a novel adaptation framework that decouples memory consumption from sequence length. While prior PEFT methods apply low-rank constraints to model parameters, LARS instead constrains the activation subspace used during training, directly targeting the dominant source of memory consumption and fundamentally flattening the memory growth rate. LARS reduces the memory footprint by an average of 33.54% on GPUs and 51.95% on CPUs in comparison to LoRA across reasoning, understanding and long-context datasets using different models while maintaining competitive accuracy and throughput. Besides GPUs, we deploy on Raspberry Pi and consumer-grade CPUs to demonstrate that LARS provides a scalable path for sophisticated LLM personalization on resource-constrained hardware and edge devices.

</details>


### [446] [Learning Without Adversarial Training: A Physics-Informed Neural Network for Secure Power System State Estimation under False Data Injection Attacks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.22784)
*Solon Falas,Markos Asprou,Charalambos Konstantinou,Maria K. Michael*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This study introduces a PINN-based model for robust state estimation in power systems, addressing stealth AC False Data Injection Attacks without adversarial training.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper aims to enhance the robustness and accuracy of state estimation in modern power systems under increasing cyber-physical security concerns due to digitalization.

Method: The authors use Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs) with dynamic loss-weighting based on homoscedastic uncertainty, avoiding adversarial training to counter stealth-constrained FDIAs.

Result: The proposed PINN-based model outperformed fixed-weight PINN variants in Mean Absolute Error evaluations for voltage magnitudes and phase angles under various attack scenarios.

Conclusion: Dynamic loss-weighting significantly improves the accuracy and robustness of PINNs for state estimation in power systems, countering stealthy cyber-physical attacks more effectively than previous approaches.

Abstract: State estimation is a cornerstone of power system control-center operations, and its robust operation is increasingly a cyber-physical security concern as modern grids become more digitalized and communication-intensive. Neural network-based approaches have gained attention as alternatives to conventional model-based state estimation methods. Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs), which embed power-flow consistency into the learning objective, have shown improved accuracy over existing approaches. This work proposes a PINN-based model for Power System State Estimation (PSSE) that protects the estimation process against the stealth-constrained AC False Data Injection Attacks (FDIAs) considered in this study. The model is developed without adversarial training. Instead, a dynamic loss-weighting formulation based on homoscedastic uncertainty learns the relative scaling of supervised data-fit and physics-residual terms during training, reducing sensitivity to manual weight tuning. Robustness is evaluated on the IEEE 118-bus system using representative stealthy-FDIA families including state distortion, load redistribution, line overloading, and residual-constrained stealth corruption. Performance is measured using Mean Absolute Error (MAE) on voltage magnitudes and phase angles. Results demonstrate higher accuracy and stability than existing fixed-weight PINN variants.

</details>


### [447] [CoFi-PGMA: Counterfactual Policy Gradients under Filtered Feedback for Multi-Agent LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.22785)
*Stela Tong,Elai Ben-Gal*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper presents CoFi-PGMA, a learning framework designed for multi-agent LLM systems that addresses the challenge of filtered feedback under routing and collaboration mechanisms.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Multi-agent LLM systems often misreceive learning signals due to collaborative or competing mechanisms, leading to misspecified training objectives with standard RLHF in such setups.

Method: The authors propose a counterfactual training objective based on marginal contributions under filtered feedback, with applications in both routing and collaborative multi-agent systems. Practical algorithms and counterfactual estimators are introduced.

Result: The framework successfully integrates counterfactual training, multiturn-aware rewards, and policy optimization to address filtered feedback issues, with validation on a real-world reasoning dataset.

Conclusion: CoFi-PGMA provides a theoretically robust and practical solution for optimizing multi-agent LLM systems by correcting misspecified learning objectives and tailoring feedback for individual agents.

Abstract: Large language model (LLM) deployments increasingly rely on multi-agent architectures in which multiple models either compete through routing mechanisms or collaborate to produce a final answer. In both settings, the learning signal received by each agent is filtered by the system mechanism. Routing produces selection-gated feedback where only the chosen response is evaluated, while collaboration produces shared rewards that obscure the individual contribution of each agent. As a result, standard RLHF objectives designed for a single deployed policy become misspecified. We introduce CoFi-PGMA (Counterfactual Policy Gradients under Filtered Feedback for Multi-Agent LLMs), a unified framework for learning under filtered feedback in multi-agent LLM systems. Our approach derives a counterfactual per-agent training objective based on marginal contribution, which corrects the learning signal under both routing and collaborative mechanisms. For routing systems, the objective corresponds to off-policy corrections for selection-gated feedback, while for collaborative systems it reduces to leave-one-out difference rewards for credit assignment. We further analyze how softmax routing induces risk-sensitive incentives and provide practical training algorithms that integrate counterfactual estimators, multiturn-aware rewards, and policy optimization methods, and demonstrate the approach on a real-world reasoning dataset.

</details>


### [448] [Robust and Clinically Reliable EEG Biomarkers: A Cross Population Framework for Generalizable Parkinson's Disease Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.23933)
*Nicholas R. Rasmussen,Longwei Wang,Rodrigue Rizk,Md Rezwanul Akter Pallab,Samuel Stuwart,Martina Mancini,Arun Singh,KC Santosh*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper addresses challenges in developing reliable EEG biomarkers for cross-population generalization in settings like Parkinson's disease detection, proposing a population-aware evaluation framework to ensure robustness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There is a need to develop EEG biomarkers that generalize well across clinical cohorts, overcoming challenges such as low signal-to-noise ratio and population-specific biases.

Method: The authors proposed a population-aware evaluation framework using an n-gram expansion strategy, with nested cross-validation for biomarker identification. The method evaluates cross-population transfer via 75 train-test configurations across five cohorts.

Result: Achieved up to 94.1% accuracy on held-out cohorts. Found that increasing training population diversity improves accuracy and stability.

Conclusion: The proposed framework offers a principled approach for developing robust and generalizable EEG biomarkers, enhancing clinical reliability in multi-site biomedical applications.

Abstract: Developing robust and clinically reliable EEG biomarkers requires evaluation frameworks that explicitly address cross population generalization in multi site settings such as Parkinsons disease (PD) detection. Models trained under i.i.d. assumptions often capture population specific artifacts rather than disease relevant neural structure, leading to poor generalization across clinical cohorts. EEG further amplifies this challenge due to low signal to noise ratio and heterogeneous acquisition conditions. We propose a population aware evaluation framework to assess the robustness and clinical reliability of EEG biomarkers under distribution shift. Using an n gram expansion strategy, we enumerate all cross population train test configurations across five independent cohorts, resulting in 75 directional evaluations. A nested cross validation design with integrated channel selection ensures prospective biomarker identification without population leakage. Results show that cross population transfer is asymmetric and that both accuracy and biomarker stability improve with increasing training population diversity, achieving up to 94.1% accuracy on held out cohorts. A theoretical analysis based on mixture risk optimization and hypothesis space contraction explains these trends, showing that multi population training promotes population robust representations. This work establishes a principled framework for learning robust, generalizable, and clinically reliable EEG biomarkers for multi site biomedical applications.

</details>


### [449] [AutoCompress: Critical Layer Isolation for Efficient Transformer Compression](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.22786)
*Archit Thorat*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: AutoCompress is a transformer compression approach focused on preserving the most critical layer (Layer 0) while compressing other layers, achieving significant parameter reduction with minimal performance loss.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the inefficiency of uniform compression methods in small transformers by leveraging an observed disproportionate importance of Layer 0 for task-critical performance.

Method: The proposed method, Critical Layer Isolation (CLI), isolates and protects the most important layer (Layer 0) at full dimensionality while compressing intermediate layers through a learned bottleneck architecture.

Result: Applied to GPT-2 Medium, CLI achieves a 2.47x compression ratio, 59.5% parameter reduction, and outperforms a uniform bottleneck compression model by achieving lower perplexity (204.5 vs. 571.8).

Conclusion: Prioritizing Layer 0 during compression significantly enhances performance, demonstrating that intelligent architectural choices surpass naive parameter reduction strategies.

Abstract: We present AutoCompress, a transformer compression method motivated by an empirical finding: in small transformers, Layer 0 carries disproportionately high task-critical information, with an NTK-based importance score of 3.6 compared to a maximum of 0.054 for all other layers -- a gap of over 60x. Based on this finding, we propose Critical Layer Isolation (CLI), an architecture that protects Layer 0 at full dimensionality, compresses all intermediate layers through a learned bottleneck, and restores the full dimension at the final layer. Applied to GPT-2 Medium (354.8M parameters), CLI-GPT2 achieves 204.5 perplexity on WikiText-103 with only 143.8M parameters -- a 2.47x compression ratio and 59.5% parameter reduction. Crucially, an ablation study demonstrates that a uniform bottleneck baseline of comparable size achieves only 571.8 perplexity under identical training conditions, confirming that the architectural decision to protect Layer 0 -- rather than simply reducing model size -- is the primary driver of performance. Code and checkpoints are publicly available.

</details>


### [450] [Conformal PM2.5 Mapping Under Spatial Covariate Shift: Satellite-Reanalysis Fusion for Africa's Green Industrial Transition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.22787)
*Yaw Osei Adjei,Davis Opoku,Ephraim Abotsi,Kwadwo Owusu Amanqua,Oliver Kornyo,Elisha Soglo-Ahianyo,Cephas Anertey Abbey*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This study develops a satellite-reanalysis PM2.5 fusion system that assesses air quality across Africa, providing crucial regional predictions and infrastructure guidance to support the continent's green industrialization goals.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper addresses the need for reliable air quality monitoring systems in Africa to support green industrialization by overcoming current infrastructure and data limitations.

Method: The study uses a fusion system combining satellite data, LightGBM, spatial cross-validation, and split conformal prediction to process and analyze PM2.5 data from 404 monitoring locations across 29 African countries.

Result: The LightGBM model achieved RMSE = 30.83, MAE = 14.54, and R2 = 0.134 with significant challenges in geographic generalization, particularly in East Africa due to covariate shifts.

Conclusion: The work introduces regional reliability flags and prioritization scores for monitoring infrastructure expansion, offering actionable insights to address Africa’s air quality challenges and align with multiple Sustainable Development Goals.

Abstract: Africa's green industrialization imperative demands reliable infrastructure for monitoring air quality. We present a satellite-reanalysis PM2.5 fusion system trained on 2,068,901 records from 404 monitoring locations in 29 African countries (OpenAQ, 2017-2022), combining LightGBM with leakage-resistant spatial cross-validation and conformal prediction to quantify predictions and their geographic applicability limits. Under 5-fold location-grouped spatial cross-validation, LightGBM achieves RMSE = 30.83 +/- 5.07 ug/m3, MAE = 14.54 +/- 1.66 ug/m3, R2 = 0.134 +/- 0.023, and macro F1 = 0.336 +/- 0.018. This R2 is substantially below random-split benchmarks (>0.90) but reflects true geographic generalisation difficulty rather than model failure. Split conformal prediction targeting 90% marginal coverage reveals severe East Africa degradation (actual PICP = 65.3% vs. nominal 90%), consistent with medium-strength covariate shift (humidity KS = 0.2237, sat_pblh KS = 0.2558). We operationalise these findings through regional reliability flags (High/Medium/Low/Unreliable) and a monitor prioritisation score directing infrastructure expansion toward highest-burden unmonitored populations, directly supporting Africa's green industrial transition and SDGs 3.9, 7.1.2, 9, 11.6.2, and 13.

</details>


### [451] [Avionic Main Fuel Pump Simulation and Fault-Diagnosis Benchmark](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.22869)
*Felix Leonhard Janzen,Lukas Moddemann,Alexander Diedrich,Oliver Niggemann*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes a high-fidelity, physics-informed co-simulation of an aircraft fuel pump system to address a lack of training data for anomaly detection and demonstrates its feasibility with machine learning techniques.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to overcome the challenge of insufficient training data for anomaly detection in critical cyber-physical systems like aircraft due to data protection issues and partial observability.

Method: The authors propose a high-fidelity co-simulation model of an aircraft fuel pump system using MATLAB/Simulink Simscape Fluids and annotate its time-series data with health and fault conditions. They also apply machine learning models, including RNN-VAE and SOM-VAE, for anomaly detection and mode discretization.

Result: The study successfully demonstrates that their co-simulation and annotated data can be used to detect anomalies and differentiate between healthy and faulty operating modes using the proposed machine learning methods.

Conclusion: The proposed simulation approach can fill the data gap in critical systems, enabling effective anomaly detection and fault diagnosis with machine learning models.

Abstract: In many cyber-physical systems, especially in critical applications such as aeroplanes, data to train anomaly detection and diagnosis algorithms is lacking due to data protection issues and partial observability. To combat this inherent lack of data, we introduce a high-fidelity, physics-informed co-simulation of a common aircraft main-fuel-pump system modelled in \textsc{MATLAB/Simulink Simscape Fluids}. We also describe its generated time-series data with health and fault mode annotations. To show feasibility of our benchmark, we apply an unsupervised Recurrent Variational Autoencoder (RNN-VAE) for anomaly detection and a SOM-VAE for operating mode discretization, trained to separate healthy and faulty conditions.

</details>


### [452] [Scaling Multi-Node Mixture-of-Experts Inference Using Expert Activation Patterns](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.23150)
*Abhimanyu Bambhaniya,Geonhwa Jeong,Jason Park,Jiecao Yu,Jaewon Lee,Pengchao Wang,Changkyu Kim,Chunqiang Tang,Tushar Krishna*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper addresses inefficiencies in Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) large language model inference due to expert load imbalance and token routing issues, proposing micro-batch grouping and expert placement strategies to improve performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The authors aimed to solve bottlenecks in MoE architectures, including expert load imbalance and high communication overhead during multi-node deployments, which hinder scalability and efficient serving.

Method: The study profiles SOTA MoE models, analyzes over 100k expert activation traces, identifies patterns in expert load and activation shifts, and proposes optimizations for grouping workloads and expert placement to enhance token locality.

Result: The proposed strategies reduced all-to-all communication by up to 20%, leading to lower decode latency and improved hardware utilization across different models and datasets.

Conclusion: The paper successfully demonstrates that workload-aware optimizations in MoE models address critical inference inefficiencies, paving the way for scalable, cost-effective deployment of large language models.

Abstract: Most recent state-of-the-art (SOTA) large language models (LLMs) use Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architectures to scale model capacity without proportional per-token compute, enabling higher-quality outputs at manageable serving costs. However, MoE inference at scale is fundamentally bottlenecked by expert load imbalance and inefficient token routing, especially in multi-node deployments where tokens are not guaranteed to be routed to local experts, resulting in significant inter-node all-to-all communication overhead.
  To systematically characterize these challenges, we profile SOTA open-source MoE models, including Llama 4 Maverick, DeepSeek V3-671B, and Qwen3-230B-A22B, on various datasets and collected over 100k real expert activation traces. Upon studying the expert activation patterns, we uncover various persistent properties across all the frontier MoE models: variable expert load imbalance, domain-specific expert activation where expert popularity shifts across task families (code, math, chat, general), and a strong correlation between prefill and decode expert activations. Motivated by these findings, we propose workload-aware micro-batch grouping and an expert placement strategy to maximize token locality to the destination expert, thereby reducing inter-node communication. Across models and datasets, these optimizations help reduce all2all communication data up to 20, resulting in lower MoE decode latency and better accelerator utilization.

</details>


### [453] [Towards Understanding the Expressive Power of GNNs with Global Readout](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.22870)
*Maurice Funk,Daumantas Kojelis*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper examines the expressive power of Aggregate-Combine-Readout Graph Neural Networks (ACR-GNNs) for first-order (FO) properties, finding cases where these networks surpass the expressive capabilities of certain logic-based systems while identifying conditions restoring characterizability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand the expressive boundaries of ACR-GNNs in relation to first-order logic properties, particularly compared to existing logical frameworks like C2.

Method: The authors focus on showing how sum aggregation and readout push GNNs beyond the limitations of C2 logic and propose two ways (limiting local aggregation or constraining to bounded-degree graphs) to align their expressive capacity with logical frameworks like graded modal logic.

Result: The paper demonstrates that ACR-GNNs can express FO properties beyond C2 and provides cases—such as bounded local aggregation or bounded-degree graphs—where they are equivalent to graded modal logic with global counting.

Conclusion: ACR-GNNs' unbounded interaction between aggregation and readout enhances their expressive power beyond C2, but logical equivalence can be retained under specific constraints.

Abstract: We study the expressive power of message-passing aggregate-combine-readout graph neural networks (ACR-GNNs). Particularly, we focus on the first-order (FO) properties expressible by this formalism. While a tight logical characterisation remains a difficult open question, we make two contributions towards answering it. First, we show that sum aggregation and readout suffice for GNNs to capture FO properties that cannot be expressed in the logic C2 on both directed and undirected graphs. This strengthens known results by Hauke and Wał{\k e}ga (2026) where aggregation and readout functions are specially crafted for the task. Second, we identify two natural ways of restoring characterisability (with regard to C2) for ACR-GNNs. One option is to limit local aggregation (without imposing restrictions on global readout), whilst the second is to run ACR-GNNs over graphs of bounded degree (but unbounded size). In both cases, the FO properties captured by GNNs are exactly those definable by a formula in graded modal logic with global counting modalities. Our results thus establish an innate lower- and upper-bound in terms of how far (fragments of) C2 can be taken to characterise GNNs, and imply that is indeed the unbounded interaction of aggregation and readout that pushes the logical expressive power of GNNs above C2.

</details>


### [454] [Efficient VQ-QAT and Mixed Vector/Linear quantized Neural Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.23172)
*Terry Gou,Puneet Gupta*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces and tests three vector quantization techniques for compressing model weights, incorporating novel methods like cosine similarity-based assignment and differentiable NAS for improvement.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The authors aim to improve VQ-based model weight compression techniques by addressing challenges of codebook collapse and exploring design trade-offs in compression methods.

Method: The method involves three techniques: (1) cosine similarity-based assignment for mitigating codebook collapse, (2) improvements to assignment using top-1 sampling and straight-through estimator, and (3) applying differentiable NAS for adaptive layer-wise quantization configuration.

Result: The approach provides insights into trade-offs and behaviors of VQ-based methods, but does not consistently outperform existing methods across all quantization levels.

Conclusion: While the method offers advancements and insights into VQ-based compression, its performance varies, highlighting the complexity and trade-offs in designing such methods.

Abstract: In this work, we developed and tested 3 techniques for vector quantization (VQ) based model weight compression. To mitigate codebook collapse and enable end-to-end training, we adopted cosine similarity-based assignment. Building on ideas from attention-based formulations in Differentiable K-Means (DKM), we further improved this approach by using cosine similarity for assignment combined with top-1 sampling and a straight-through estimator, thereby eliminating the need for weighted-average reconstruction. Finally, we investigated the use of differentiable neural architecture search (NAS) to adaptively select layer-wise quantization configurations, further optimizing the compression process. Although our method does not consistently outperform existing approaches across all quantization levels, it provides useful insights into the design trade-offs and behaviors of VQ-based model compression methods.

</details>


### [455] [Cortex-Inspired Continual Learning: Unsupervised Instantiation and Recovery of Functional Task Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.24637)
*Kevin McKee,Thomas Hazy,Yicong Zheng,Zacharie Bugaud,Thomas Miconi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces Functional Task Networks (FTN), a parameter-isolation method to address catastrophic forgetting in block-sequential continual learning without relying on task labels. It achieves this through self-organizing binary masks and efficient sub-network recovery.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper aims to tackle the challenge where a model must prevent catastrophic forgetting and infer task-relevant solutions autonomously during continual learning without external task labels.

Method: FTN employs a self-organizing binary mask inspired by neocortical motifs. It uses a three-stage process: gradient descent on continuous masks to identify neurons, smoothing for spatial contiguity, and k-winner-take-all binarization within a fixed budget.

Result: FTN effectively prevents forgetting and enables task recovery across diverse benchmarks like synthetic tasks, MNIST variants, and Permuted MNIST. FTN-Slow emphasizes retention while FTN-Fast balances speed and some forgetting.

Conclusion: FTN reduces computational complexity, avoids catastrophic forgetting, and provides robust unsupervised task segmentation in block-sequential continual learning scenarios.

Abstract: Block-sequential continual learning demands that a single model both protect prior solutions from catastrophic forgetting and efficiently infer at inference time which prior solution matches the current input without task labels. We present Functional Task Networks (FTN), a parameter-isolation method inspired by structural and dynamical motifs found in the mammalian neocortex. Similar to mixture-of-experts, this method uses a high dimensional, self-organizing binary mask over a large population of small but deep networks, inspired by dendritic models of pyramidal neurons. The mask is produced by a three-stage procedure: (1) gradient descent on a continuous mask identifies task-relevant neurons, (2) a smoothing kernel biases the result toward spatial contiguity, (3) and k-winner-take-all binarizes the resulting group at a fixed capacity budget. Like mixture-of-experts, each neuron is an independent deep network, so disjoint masks give exactly disjoint gradient updates, providing structural guarantees against catastrophic forgetting. This three-stage procedure recovers the sub-network of a previously-trained task in a single gradient step, providing unsupervised task segmentation at inference time. We test it on three continual-learning benchmarks: (1) a synthetic multi-task classification/regression generator, (2) MNIST with shuffled class labels (pure concept shift), and (3) Permuted MNIST (domain shift). On all three, FTN with fine grained smoothing (FTN-Slow) results in nearly zero forgetting. FTN with a large kernel and only 2 iterations of smoothing (FTN-Fast) trades off some retention for increased speed. We show that the spatial organization mechanism reduces the effective mask search from the combinatorial top-k subset problem in O(C(H,K)) to the complexity of a near-linear scan in O(H) over compact cortical neighborhoods, which is parallelized by the gradient-based update.

</details>


### [456] [When Policies Cannot Be Retrained: A Unified Closed-Form View of Post-Training Steering in Offline Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.22873)
*Elias Hossain,Mohammad Jahid Ibna Basher,Ivan Garibay,Ozlem Garibay,Niloofar Yousefi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Researchers study deployment-time adaptation for offline RL using Product-of-Experts composition. PoE demonstrates robust performance anchored to frozen actors, while other methods often degrade significantly.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Deployment objectives may change after offline RL training, but retraining may not be feasible due to cost, data availability, or governance limitations.

Method: Using PoE composition and goal-conditioned priors, offline RL policies are adapted for deployment, comparing their effectiveness to additive and prior-only approaches.

Result: PoE adapts gracefully under degraded or random priors, avoiding performance collapse, and achieves near-oracle outcomes with a KL-budget selector. It outperforms other composition methods across varied environments.

Conclusion: PoE and KL-regularized adaptation serve as robust mechanisms for deployment-time steering of offline RL actors without retraining, though their effectiveness is bounded by actor competence and task difficulty.

Abstract: Offline reinforcement learning (RL) can learn effective policies from fixed datasets, but deployment objectives may change after training, and in many applications the trained actor cannot be retrained because of data, cost, or governance constraints. We study deployment-time adaptation for frozen offline actors using Product-of-Experts (PoE) composition with a goal-conditioned prior. Our main practical finding is graceful degradation rather than universal performance gain: under degraded or random priors, precision-weighted composition remains anchored to the frozen actor, while additive and prior-only adaptation collapse, and a KL-budget selector often recovers a near-oracle operating point. We also make explicit a closed-form identity in the frozen-actor setting: for diagonal-Gaussian actors and priors, PoE with coefficient alpha yields the same deterministic policy as KL-regularized adaptation with beta = alpha / (1 - alpha), with posterior covariances differing only by a global scalar factor. Empirically, across four D4RL environments (3,900 MuJoCo episodes), we observe a 4/5/3 HELP/FROZEN/HURT split. Extending the analysis to six harder cells and two AntMaze diagnostics reveals an actor-competence ceiling: medium-expert remains HURT in all 9 cells at every tested alpha, while AntMaze with a behavior-cloned frozen actor yields zero success for all composition rules. Overall, PoE and KL-regularized adaptation are best viewed as a single actor-anchored safety mechanism for deployment-time steering.

</details>


### [457] [MTServe: Efficient Serving for Generative Recommendation Models with Hierarchical Caches](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.22881)
*Xin Wang,Chi Ma,Shaobin Chen,Pu Wang,Menglei Zhou,Junyi Qiu,Qiaorui Chen,Jiayu Sun,Shijie Liu,Zehuan Wang,Lei Yu,Chuan Liu,Fei Jiang,Wei Lin,Hao Wang,Jiawei Jiang,Xiao Yan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes MTServe, a cache management system, to reduce the costs of generative recommendation by optimizing storage and data transfer.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation stems from the efficiency challenges in generative recommendations due to costly repeated encoding and storage issues.

Method: MTServe hierarchically manages GPU memory and RAM, combined with specific optimizations like hybrid layouts, asynchronous transfer, and locality-driven policies.

Result: MTServe achieves up to 3.1x speedup and a hit ratio over 98.5% when tested on both public and production datasets.

Conclusion: MTServe significantly optimizes the performance of generative recommendation models by reducing inference cost and managing large-scale user states efficiently.

Abstract: Generative recommendation (GR) offers superior modeling capabilities but suffers from prohibitive inference costs due to the repeated encoding of long user histories. While cross-request Key-Value (KV) cache reuse presents a significant optimization opportunity, the massive scale of individual user states creates a storage explosion that far exceeds physical GPU limits. We propose MTServe, a hierarchical cache management system that virtualizes GPU memory by leveraging host RAM as a scalable backup store. To bridge the I/O gap between tiers, MTServe introduces a suite of system-level optimizations, including a hybrid storage layout, an asynchronous data transfer pipeline, and a locality-driven replacement policy. On both public and production datasets, MTServe delivers up to 3.1* speedup while maintaining near-perfect hit ratios (>98.5%).

</details>


### [458] [FlashOverlap: Minimizing Tail Latency in Communication Overlap for Distributed LLM Training](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.24013)
*Rezaul Karim,Austin Wen,Wang Zongzuo,Weiwei Zhang,Yang Liu,Walid Ahmed*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces Flash-Overlap, a novel method to address communication-computation bottlenecks in distributed training of large language models, achieving lower latency and higher throughput.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing parallelization strategies for large language models suffer from high communication overhead and tail latency, hindering computational efficiency during distributed training.

Method: Flash-Overlap employs decomposed peer-to-peer communication and schedules partitioned computations to enable fine-grained communication-computation overlap, replacing traditional collective operations like reduce-scatter and all-gather.

Result: The method eliminates tail latency in distributed training, enhances Model FLOPS Utilization (MFU), lowers latency, and achieves higher throughput, proving effective across various parallel strategies.

Conclusion: Flash-Overlap provides a versatile and efficient solution to communication bottlenecks in distributed training of large-scale models, offering improvements in latency, throughput, and computational efficiency.

Abstract: The rapid growth in the size of large language models has necessitated the partitioning of computational workloads across accelerators such as GPUs, TPUs, and NPUs. However, these parallelization strategies incur substantial data communication overhead significantly hindering computational efficiency. While communication-computation overlap presents a promising direction, existing data slicing based solutions suffer from tail latency. To overcome this limitation, this research introduces a novel communication-computation overlap technique to eliminate this tail latency in state of the art overlap methods for distributed LLM training. The aim of this technique is to effectively mitigate communication bottleneck of tensor parallelism and data parallelism for distributed training and inference. In particular, we propose a novel method termed Flash-Overlap that replaces conventional collective operations of reduce-scatter and all-gather with decomposed peer-to-peer (P2P) communication and schedules partitioned computations to enable fine-grained overlap. Our method provides an exact algorithm for reducing communication overhead that eliminates tail latency. Moreover, it presents a versatile solution compatible with data-parallel training and various tensor-level parallelism strategies, including TPSP and UP. Experimental evaluations demonstrate that our technique consistently achieves lower latency, superior Model FLOPS Utilization (MFU), and high throughput.

</details>


### [459] [Evaluating CUDA Tile for AI Workloads on Hopper and Blackwell GPUs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.23466)
*Divakar Kumar Yadav,Tian Zhao,Deepak Kumar*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper evaluates NVIDIA's Python-based CUDA Tile (CuTile) for GPU kernel development, emphasizing its performance and portability across AI workloads and GPU architectures.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation for this paper is to independently benchmark and assess the effectiveness and portability of CuTile in simplifying GPU programming while maintaining high performance on modern GPUs.

Method: The researchers conducted cross-architecture evaluations, benchmarking CuTile against approaches like cuBLAS, Triton, and FlashAttention-2 across AI workloads (GEMM, fused multi-head attention, LLM inference) on three NVIDIA GPUs.

Result: Results show that CuTile achieves impressive performance in specific cases (e.g., 1007 TFLOP/s for fused attention on B200) but suffers from substantial cross-architecture optimization gaps, demonstrating poor portability compared to Triton.

Conclusion: While CuTile simplifies programming and performs well for certain workloads, its lack of optimization for diverse architectures limits its practicality. In contrast, tools like Triton are more portable without requiring extensive tuning.

Abstract: NVIDIA's CUDA Tile (CuTile) introduces a Python-based, tile-centric abstraction for GPU kernel development that aims to simplify programming while retaining Tensor Core and Tensor Memory Accelerator (TMA) efficiency on modern GPUs. We present the first independent, cross-architecture evaluation of CuTile against established approaches such as cuBLAS, Triton, WMMA, and raw SIMT on three NVIDIA GPUs spanning Hopper and Blackwell: H100 NVL, B200, and RTX PRO 6000 Blackwell Server Edition. We benchmark representative AI workloads, including GEMM, fused multi-head attention, and end-to-end LLM inference in BF16/FP16 precision, to assess both performance and portability.
  Our results show that CuTile effectiveness is strongly workload- and architecture-dependent. On datacenter-class Blackwell (B200), CuTile achieves up to 1007 TFLOP/s for fused attention, outperforming FlashAttention-2 by 2.5x while requiring only 60 lines of Python kernel code. For GEMM, CuTile reaches 52-79% of cuBLAS performance in 22 lines of code (versus 123 for WMMA), making it a practical replacement for hand-written CUDA kernels but not yet for vendor-optimized libraries. However, the same CuTile attention kernel achieves only 53% of FlashAttention-2 throughput on RTX PRO 6000 (sm_120), exposing significant cross-architecture optimization gaps. In contrast, Triton sustains 62-101% of cuBLAS performance across all tested platforms without architecture-specific tuning, demonstrating substantially stronger portability.

</details>


### [460] [Predicting Wind Loads on Container Ships in Harbor Environments through Multi-Fidelity Modeling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.22882)
*Matilde Fiore,Andrea Bresciani,Miguel Alfonso Mendez,Jeroen van Beeck*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper develops a multi-fidelity surrogate model to improve prediction accuracy of wind loads on modern container ships, effectively fusing empirical data with CFD simulations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Accurate wind load prediction is critical for mooring design in modern container ships, but existing models are outdated and inaccurate, especially for larger vessels and complex environments.

Method: The study introduces a multi-fidelity surrogate modelling framework using recursive co-kriging to combine empirical correlations with detailed and simplified CFD models, alongside sensitivity analysis and sequential sampling for efficient parameter exploration.

Result: The multi-fidelity model significantly improves prediction accuracy while minimizing computational costs, outperforming traditional empirical methods and single-fidelity models.

Conclusion: The proposed framework provides a robust and computationally efficient tool for predicting wind loads on container ships, addressing limitations of existing approaches and enhancing engineering applications.

Abstract: Modern container ships face higher wind loads due to increased windage areas, making accurate predictions of wind loads essential for mooring design. Existing empirical models, largely developed for container ships with smaller windage areas and simpler geometrical configurations than those of modern large-scale vessels, often lack accuracy and do not account for the influence of nearby structures. This study proposes a multi-fidelity surrogate modelling framework for the prediction of wind-load coefficients, combining empirical correlations with simplified and detailed CFD models for ships in open-sea and harbor environments. The approach relies on recursive co-kriging to consistently fuse information across fidelity levels, enabling accurate predictions at a reduced computational cost. A sensitivity analysis is used to identify the most influential geometric parameters, and the resulting reduced parameter space is explored through sequential sampling to efficiently construct the training database.
  The surrogate models are validated over a wide range of loading configurations and for two distinct harbor environments. The results demonstrate that the multi-fidelity approach significantly improves prediction accuracy compared to single-fidelity models, while substantially reducing the reliance on high-fidelity simulations. In particular, the proposed framework captures the dependence of wind loads on key geometric parameters and consistently outperforms traditional empirical correlations, providing a robust and efficient tool for engineering applications.

</details>


### [461] [FreeScale: Distributed Training for Sequence Recommendation Models with Minimal Scaling Cost](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.24073)
*Chenhao Feng,Haoli Zhang,Shakhzod Ali-Zade,Yanli Zhao,Liang Luo,Jennifer Cao,Lisen Deng,Siqiao Chen,Chenyu Zhao,Tristan Rice,Daniel Johnson,Min Si,Tiantu Xu,Yi Zhang,Siqi Yan,Chuanhao Zhuge,Min Ni,Bi Xue,Qunshu Zhang,Shen Li*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces FreeScale, a technique to optimize resource utilization in industrial Deep Learning Recommendation Models by addressing stragglers, blocking communication, and GPU resource competition.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to tackle challenges in large-scale training of Deep Learning Recommendation Models caused by computational inefficiencies, stragglers, and communication bottlenecks.

Method: FreeScale employs methods such as load balancing for input samples, overlapping communication with computation, and SM-Free techniques to minimize resource competition and improve efficiency.

Result: Empirical evaluation with 256 H100 GPUs showed that FreeScale reduces computational bubbles by up to 90.3%.

Conclusion: FreeScale effectively optimizes computational resource utilization, enhancing the performance of large-scale Deep Learning Recommendation Models.

Abstract: Modern industrial Deep Learning Recommendation Models typically extract user preferences through the analysis of sequential interaction histories, subsequently generating predictions based on these derived interests. The inherent heterogeneity in data characteristics frequently result in substantial under-utilization of computational resources during large-scale training, primarily due to computational bubbles caused by severe stragglers and slow blocking communications. This paper introduces FreeScale, a solution designed to (1) mitigate the straggler problem through meticulously load balanced input samples (2) minimize the blocking communication by overlapping prioritized embedding communications with computations (3) resolve the GPU resource competition during computation and communication overlapping by communicating through SM-Free techniques. Empirical evaluation demonstrates that FreeScale achieves up to 90.3% reduction in computational bubbles when applied to real-world workloads running on 256 H100 GPUs.

</details>


### [462] [Hybrid JIT-CUDA Graph Optimization for Low-Latency Large Language Model Inference](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.23467)
*Divakar Kumar Yadav,Tian Zhao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces a hybrid runtime framework combining JIT compilation and CUDA Graph execution to reduce inference latency for large language models (LLMs).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current LLMs face the challenge of high inference latency and kernel launch overhead, especially for interactive tasks involving short sequences.

Method: The framework splits transformer inference into static (handled by CUDA Graph replay) and dynamic (handled through JIT-compiled kernels) components, enabling asynchronous graph capture and reuse during autoregressive decoding.

Result: The proposed approach, evaluated on LLaMA-2 7B, achieved up to 66.0% reduction in Time-to-First-Token (TTFT) and lower P99 latency in single-GPU, batch-size-one inference across varying prompt lengths.

Conclusion: Hybrid JIT-CUDA Graph execution effectively reduces inference latency and variance for short-sequence LLM tasks, making it valuable for latency-sensitive AI applications.

Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) have achieved strong performance across natural language and multimodal tasks, yet their practical deployment remains constrained by inference latency and kernel launch overhead, particularly in interactive, short-sequence settings. This paper presents a hybrid runtime framework that combines Just-In-Time (JIT) compilation with CUDA Graph execution to reduce launch overhead while preserving runtime flexibility during autoregressive decoding. The framework partitions transformer inference into static components executed via CUDA Graph replay and dynamic components handled through JIT-compiled kernels, enabling asynchronous graph capture and reuse across decoding steps.
  We evaluate the proposed approach on LLaMA-2 7B using single-GPU, batch-size-one inference across prompt lengths from 10 to 500 tokens. Experimental results show that the hybrid runtime reduces Time-to-First-Token (TTFT) by up to 66.0% and achieves lower P99 latency compared with TensorRT-LLM in this regime. These results indicate that hybrid JIT-CUDA Graph execution can effectively reduce inference latency and variance for short-sequence LLM workloads, making it a practical optimization strategy for latency-sensitive AI applications.

</details>


### [463] [Quantifying and Mitigating Self-Preference Bias of LLM Judges](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.22891)
*Jinming Yang,Chuxian Qiu,Zhenyu Deng,Xinshan Jiao,Tao Zhou*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper identifies and addresses Self-Preference Bias (SPB) in LLM-based automated evaluation systems, proposing an automated framework to measure and mitigate it, reducing bias by 31.5%.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Self-Preference Bias (SPB) distorts the reliability of LLMs in automated evaluation systems, and current methods to measure it are impractical due to reliance on costly human annotations and conflated factors.

Method: The authors developed an automated framework to measure SPB by constructing response pairs with negligible quality differences, separating bias from discriminative capability. They also propose a multi-dimensional evaluation strategy to reduce SPB, based on cognitive load decomposition.

Result: Empirical analysis across 20 popular LLMs showed no correlation or negative correlation between advanced model capabilities and lower SPB. The proposed strategy reduced SPB by 31.5% on average.

Conclusion: The study highlights SPB as a critical challenge in LLM evaluations and offers a practical automated method to quantify and mitigate this bias, improving the trustworthiness of LLM judges.

Abstract: LLM-as-a-Judge has become a dominant approach in automated evaluation systems, playing critical roles in model alignment, leaderboard construction, quality control, and so on. However, the scalability and trustworthiness of this approach can be substantially distorted by Self-Preference Bias (SPB), which is a directional evaluative deviation in which LLMs systematically favor or disfavor their own generated outputs during evaluation. Existing measurements rely on costly human annotations and conflate generative capability with evaluative stance, and thus are impractical for large-scale deployment in real-world systems. To address this issue, we introduce a fully automated framework to quantifying and mitigating SPB, which constructs equal-quality pairs of responses with negligible quality differences, enabling statistical disentanglement of discriminability from bias propensity without human gold standards. Empirical analysis across 20 mainstream LLMs reveals that advanced capabilities are often uncorrelated, or even negatively correlated, with low SPB. To mitigate this bias, we propose a structured multi-dimensional evaluation strategy grounded in cognitive load decomposition, which reduces SPB by 31.5\% on average.

</details>


### [464] [StackFeat RL: Reinforcement Learning over Iterative Dual Criterion Feature Selection for Stable Biomarker Discovery](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.22892)
*A. Yermekov,D. A. Herrera-Martí*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: StackFeat-RL is proposed as a meta-learning framework for feature selection in high-dimensional genomic data, achieving high accuracy, sparsity, and stability compared to existing methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address limitations of existing genomic feature selection methods, such as manual thresholds, instability under data perturbation, and disregard for biological structure.

Method: StackFeat-RL employs REINFORCE-based policy gradients to tune hyperparameters of a dual-criterion iterative feature selection algorithm that emphasizes coefficient consistency and selection frequency.

Result: StackFeat-RL outperforms traditional methods in predictive accuracy and achieves sparsity, requiring 3-4 times fewer features on COVID-19 and Alzheimer's genomic datasets.

Conclusion: The method shows promise for biomarker discovery in high-dimensional genomic settings, demonstrating both practical utility and theoretical robustness.

Abstract: Feature selection in high-dimensional genomic data ($d \gg n$) demands methods that are simultaneously accurate, sparse, and stable.
